abstract: The normative framework that defines ideal motherhood, prescribing what it means to be a “good mother” in a given cultural and historical context, significantly influences women’s career aspirations, family choices, and fertility decisions. Yet, there is limited understanding of what women themselves consider to be “ideal motherhood”, particularly given that such ideals may reflect both genuine preferences and adaptations to structural constraints. Gaining insight into both personal and perceived societal ideals is crucial for evaluating the extent to which women’s views of motherhood align with, or diverge from, prevailing social expectations. This study builds on hegemonic and non-hegemonic models of motherhood differing in the degree of maternal involvement, the role of maternal employment, and the division of childcare responsibilities. We conduct a large-scale conjoint experiment embedded in an online survey administered in autumn 2024, involving approximately 4,500 Italian women aged 20 to 50, to evaluate the relative importance of various attributes associated with different models of motherhood. The findings show that women tend to favor elements of counter-hegemonic motherhood models over hegemonic ones, both in their personal ideals and in their perceptions of societal expectations. In particular, maternal employment (whether full- or part-time) and the sharing of childcare responsibilities with fathers emerge as central components of the ideal mother. These results challenge conventional assumptions about women’s endorsement of the dominant notion of mothering, offering new insight into shifting motherhood ideals in contemporary Italy.

title: The Preprint Problem Problem: Response to Giangrande
abstract: Giangrande (2026) argues that fringe scientists have “weaponized” preprints to spread pseudoscience, and presents data to prove the point. In a response, I identify two major problems in the paper. The first is that the data do not match their description, and the second is that description of how preprint servers work – and thus the mechanism of action for the problem – is inaccurate. In addition, the documentation provided with the paper is inadequate for assessment. The paper does not establish the prevalence of weaponized pseudoscience on preprint servers beyond an existence proof. I conclude that the paper cannot live up to its inflammatory title, “The Preprint Problem” – but scientists do have an important role to play in protecting our communities from the bad actors of pseudoscience.

title: Intergroup Violence Without Essentialist Beliefs: The Case of Turkana Cattle Raiders
abstract: Essentialism has been hypothesized to be associated with intergroup hostility. However, it is unclear whether ethnic essentialism is a feature of human social cognition across a range of socio-ecological settings and what the causal direction of its association with intergroup hostility is. One possibility is that essentialist beliefs cause intergroup violence because they make violence on members of out-groups more palatable. An alternative hypothesis is that essentialist beliefs may develop as a response to intergroup violence. Using “switched at birth” vignettes to assess how intergenerationally inherited and fixed people’s identities are, we assessed the extent to which Turkana pastoralist warriors have essentialist beliefs about themselves and the neighboring Toposa ethnic group in South Sudan. The Turkana and Toposa engage in mutually lethal cattle raids, which account for about half of adult Turkana male mortality in our study area. We found that only four of 162 (2.5%) Turkana surveyed expressed essentialist beliefs about themselves and the Toposa, suggesting that ethnic essentialism may not be a cross-culturally widespread feature of social cognition nor a necessary ideology for engaging in intergroup hostilities. The four participants who expressed essentialist beliefs did not exhibit unusual combat-related behavior and attitudes towards the outgroup. Specifically, they were no different than non-essentialist survey respondents in their levels of combat exposure, killing of enemies in combat, witnessing morally injurious events, and moral judgments about killing Toposa. However, one of the four was an outlier in offensive raiding and killing, anecdotally suggesting that essentialism may contribute to, but be insufficient for, very prolific raiding in individual Turkana. The cultural similarity between the Toposa and Turkana, a common origin story, well-known examples of cross-group adoption and assimilation, and a tradition of cross-border friendships may explain the Turkana’s low frequency of essentialist beliefs despite widespread lethal violence.

title: Peace, War, and the Spirit of Commerce: Genovesi as a reader of Montesquieu
abstract: What are the consequences of trade among nations? In De l’esprit des lois, Montesquieu claimed that the spirit of commerce fosters peace, restraining conflict by leading nations to collaborate in pursuit of mutual benefit. Economic interests, he suggested, channel and moderate the violent impulses of human passions. In contrast, the Neapolitan philosopher and economist Antonio Genovesi offered a strikingly different interpretation. In his reading of Montesquieu, Genovesi argued that the spirit of commerce was itself a spirit of conquest—an engine of rivalry and domination among nations. Yet, he maintained, the telos of trade rests upon an alternative spirit, one of mutual aid, capable of nurturing peace and cooperation both within and between societies. This paper offers a philosophical analysis of the dialogue between Montesquieu and Genovesi, tracing how their exchange shaped Genovesi’s evolving reflections on the intricate relationship between commerce and war.

title: Negotiating the Good Mother Ideal: Maternal Guilt in Contemporary Society
authors: Maria-Valentina Stoicescu, Natalia Soboleva, Nevena Kulic, Giulia Dotti Sani
abstract: This narrative review integrates quantitative and qualitative research to provide a comprehensive overview of the phenomenon of maternal guilt, examining its measurement, types, triggers, and prevalence. Maternal guilt is a widespread emotion that is associated with the societal role of women as caregivers, yet this association is sometimes lost when talking more broadly about parental guilt. Critical gaps persist in the understanding and measurement of maternal guilt, particularly in the areas of cross-cultural variation, intersectional perspectives, and the long-term consequences for both mothers and their children. The classification of maternal guilt into types highlights that maternal guilt can take different forms, depending on specific root causes and circumstances. By analysing triggers, this review also shows that maternal guilt is enhanced through individual, situational, and institutional factors, and it varies across countries, being influenced by societal and cultural characteristics.

title: Using Statistical Modelling and Geographic Data to Challenge Declinist Headlines - a Case Study of Fish and Chip Shops in Great Britain
abstract: The traditional fish and chip shop is an important part of the food environment in many UK neighbourhoods. However, rising costs, changing tastes and competition have resulted in fish and chip shop closures, with some alarmist predictions that up to half of all shops could close in a single year. Using Points of Interest data and multilevel modelling, we look beyond the headlines and model trends in fish and chip shop provision by neighbourhood type between 2022 and 2025. We identify an overall decline in the number of shops yet find no evidence to support the predictions of widespread closures. However, closures have been unevenly spread across neighbourhood types, with the greatest decline in deprived and ethnically diverse urban areas, and less impact on some more affluent neighbourhoods with older populations. Although some adaption may be needed to maintain viable amidst rising costs, our findings suggest that the traditional fish and chip shop remains an important part of the food environment in many neighbourhoods. In conclusion, we highlight the importance of robust data driven insight when considering the changing provision of services and comment on how these approaches could be applied in other contexts.

title: Teaching Teachers
authors: Danilo Kuzmanic, Per Engzell, John Jerrim
abstract: Good teachers are role models. Using Chilean administrative data, we estimate how teacher value-added (VA) affects the VA of students who later become teachers. Exposure to a math teacher with 1 SD higher VA raises teacher VA in the next generation by 0.14 SD and student achievement one generation removed by 0.04 SD. Effects compound with longer exposure and appear driven by observable classroom practices. Under plausible assumptions, these spillovers raise cumulative returns to instructional quality by 28 percent after one generation and 39 percent in the long run.

title: Industry Differences in Upward Mobility from Low-Wage Labour Market Entry
abstract: A low-wage occupation is a common entry point into the labour market, yet the trajectories that follow vary considerably. This study examines whether patterns of upward mobility from low-wage labour market entry differ across industries. Using Swedish full population register data, the analysis follows all individuals aged 18–25 who entered the labour market in 2003 in low wage occupations and traces their occupational trajectories over 18 years. Event history models are used to estimate the timing of transitions from low wage to higher wage occupations. The results indicate substantial and persistent differences across industries in both the pace and likelihood of upward mobility. Workers in manufacturing and construction tend to move into higher wage occupations more quickly and more often, whereas those in hospitality, cleaning, and personal services experience markedly slower transitions. In several of these service industries, workers remain in low wage occupations more than twice as long as comparable workers in manufacturing, and a sizeable share do not transition at all within the observation period. As these service industries expand their share of low wage jobs—and as women and immigrants remain disproportionately concentrated within them—these patterns raise concerns about social stratification. The findings highlight how industrial structures shape early career mobility and interact with broader labour market inequalities, underscoring the importance of industrial opportunity structures for understanding trajectories from low-wage work.

title: Between the Embedding and the Prompt: Systematic Design Effects in LLM-based Occupation Coding
authors: Olga Kononykhina, Anna-Carolina Haensch, Frauke Kreuter
abstract: Assigning free-text job descriptions to standardised taxonomies is a persistent bottleneck in survey research and official statistics. Large language models (LLMs) offer a promising path toward automation, but each step in the pipeline involves both model architecture and measurement choices about how an occupation should be represented. Through 119 experiments on German survey data, we systematically vary the textual representation of occupational categories, embedding models, LLMs, and prompt design. Category representation changes retrieval accuracy by 8–23 percentage points and classification by 11–21. Prompt role and abstention behaviour are model-specific and must be validated before deployment. The dominant source of variance, however, sits outside model measurement choices. How respondents describe their work matters more than any model or design choice (ICC = 0.76).

title: TOWARD MORPHOLOGICALLY-AWARE TOKENIZATION FOR INFLECTIONAL LANGUAGES: A STRUCTURAL HYPOTHESIS
abstract: This article proposes a theoretical hypothesis concerning the structural and temporal architecture of tokenization in inflectional languages. The starting point is an observation documented in prior empirical work (Dawidek, 2026a; 2026b): standard frequency-based tokenization systematically fails to preserve morphologically significant boundaries in Polish, a synthetic inflectional language. The present article argues that this failure has a deeper cause than training data composition or algorithm choice. Standard tokenization has no preconditions — it begins cutting without structural knowledge of the word — and no concept of time: it treats the interval between cuts as empty latency rather than as a potential carrier of morphological information. I propose two interconnected hypotheses. First, that linguistically motivated tokenization requires a morphological skeleton as a precondition — a minimal representation of root, prefix, ending type, case, number, gender, and valency, specified here for Polish and hypothesized to generalize to other inflectional languages. Second, that the cutting order itself is morphologically motivated and temporally structured: prefix first, inflectional ending second, root exposed last — where exposure is not a cut but a revelation of what was present throughout. I further propose the concept of morphological resolution time — the interval between cuts understood as a site of grammatical resolution rather than computational waste. This proposal has architectural consequences: the temporal dimension required by the hypothesis is structurally incompatible with standard transformer parallelism. A hybrid architecture combining sequential morphological pre-processing with parallel downstream processing is identified as a research direction. This article presents a hypothesis, not demonstrated results. It is offered as a theoretical framework for empirical work that has not yet been done — and as a named set of variables that must exist before measurement can begin.

title: Social Entropy, Adaptive Coherence, and Transcendent Detachment: An Integrative Framework for Measuring Coherence and Fragility in Human Systems
abstract: Human groups, institutions, relationships, and identities can be modeled as dynamic information-processing systems that require continual maintenance to pre serve coherence under uncertainty. This article develops Adaptive Coherence and Entropic Attachment (ACEA), an integrative framework linking information theory, cybernetics, resilience science, attachment theory, stress appraisal, predictive processing, allostasis, discourse analysis, and institutional theory. The framework formalizes social coherence as a time-dependent state variable shaped by trust, shared knowledge, relational cohesion, communication clarity, adaptive capacity, regenerative repair, informational noise, fear, uncertainty, external disturbance, and attachment rigidity. The central contribution is a testable account of “transcendent detachment” as adaptive non-rigidity: the capacity to preserve meaning, commitment, and identity without requiring absolute permanence or total control. ACEA is not presented as a finished empirical law, but as a formal research program that generates measurable constructs, falsifiable hypotheses, and practical applications for discourse analysis, organizational resilience, conflict mediation, institutional design, and psychological adaptation.

title: The Origin of Patriarchal Moral System: An Evolutionary Model
abstract: [This is a new submission replacing a previous version that was accidentally deleted. The earlier submission was declined due to an incomplete ORCID profile; the profile has now been updated with full professional information, qualifications, and biography. The manuscript is currently under peer review at the journal Human Nature and is not AI-generated.] This article proposes an evolutionary-materialist model for the origin of the patriarchal moral system — a set of normative prohibitions restricting divorce, female sexuality, and freedom of mate choice. The model takes as its starting point the reproductive strategy of male provisioning — the exchange of male‑provided resources for female reproductive access — shaped by sexual selection. It is shown that the transition from foraging to civilization generates three demographic problems that make this strategy unstable: (a) increased population density facilitating partner switching, (b) awareness of the causal link between intercourse and childbirth reducing female motivation for sex, and (c) dissociation between the timing of sexual and social maturity in males. The normative solution to each of these problems—and their combination as a patriarchal moral system—is proposed to be understood as an adaptation to new demographic conditions. The model explains the convergent emergence of similar prohibitions across different cultures and the specific role of older males in enforcing them. The article is theoretical in nature and can serve as a foundation for further empirical research.

title: Naming under constraint: gender, care and symbolic power in family life
abstract: This article proposes a reconceptualisation of children’s surname attribution as a gendered site of symbolic power within heterosexual family life. While naming is commonly framed as a private and consensual decision, feminist scholarship has shown that family practices are structured by persistent asymmetries of care, authority, and recognition. Yet the symbolic dimension of naming remains underexamined. Building on feminist theories of care, symbolic violence, and situated knowledge, this article introduces the concept of naming under constraint to analyse how naming decisions are produced under conditions that limit deliberation and agency. Drawing on a case in Portugal, it shows how a naming decision taken immediately after childbirth - under bodily vulnerability, institutional urgency, and unequal care relations - becomes stabilised as a consensual outcome. The article further develops the notion of delayed articulation to theorise the temporal gap between living inequality and naming it as such, highlighting how care-intensive life stages constrain feminist critique. By shifting attention from naming as a neutral outcome to naming as a situated process, this article contributes to sociological debates on gender, care, and symbolic power, and highlights the importance of attending to the temporal and relational conditions under which inequality becomes intelligible.

title: The Silence of Moderates Drives Social Polarization: An Agent-Based Model of Perceived and Actual Polarization
abstract: Social polarization increasingly undermines the collaborative dialogue essential to democratic societies, necessitating a clearer understanding of the mechanisms behind its emergence and resolution. Traditional research has explained polarization through interpersonal repulsion, in which individuals move their opinions away from different views (e.g., Axelrod et al., 2021). While this framework focuses on conflicts among active speakers, it overlooks the silence and withdrawal of moderates. We developed a model that excludes repulsion and incorporates the silence gap effect, in which individuals remain silent toward those with stronger attitudes. Simulations show that in the early stages, the silence gap effect causes moderates to remain silent while allowing extreme opinions to dominate public expressions. This generates perceived polarization, a state in which the distribution of public opinion becomes bimodal despite unimodal internal attitudes. Subsequently, the frequently expressed extreme opinions pull other agents toward the poles, eventually leading to polarization. These findings demonstrate that the essence of polarization lies in the silence of moderates. Furthermore, our study shows that polarization can be prevented by neutralizing the silence gap effect, ensuring that moderates do not suppress opinions. However, we found that even slight differences in intervention timing determined whether future polarization was successfully averted. By contrast, interventions aimed at correcting the misperception of a polarized public failed to prevent division. Therefore, to resolve polarization, it is vital to create a dialogue environment in which moderates can speak as early as possible without apprehension, rather than directly addressing extreme voices or promoting the correction of public perception.

title: Image Abstraction and Bayesian Inference: A Reconstruction of the Nature of Probability Theory from the Perspective of Cognitive Philosophy
abstract: This paper demonstrates, from an epistemological standpoint, the structural isomorphism between Quxiang Bilei—the classical Chinese cognitive method of image abstraction and analogical reasoning, literally "taking phenomena to form analogies and categorical associations"—and Bayesian inference. By systematically mapping the three components of Bayes's theorem—prior probability, likelihood function, and posterior probability—onto the three constitutive stages of Quxiang Bilei: image-taking, analogy-making, and verification, this paper reveals that Bayesian inference is not merely a specialized technical toolset, but rather the mathematical formalization of the metacognitive structure inherent in Quxiang Bilei. Building upon this isomorphic framework, the paper advances five core philosophical propositions: (1) objectivity is the statistical convergence outcome of collective Bayesian updating across epistemic communities; (2) causal laws represent the limiting case in which posterior probability infinitely approaches unity; (3) the interpretability of probabilistic calculations resides not within mathematics itself, but in its correspondence with underlying metacognitive structures; (4) the absolute boundary of artificial intelligence lies not in insufficient computational power, but in its lack of embodied physical anchoring and authentic survival feedback; and (5) there exists a fundamental, non-accidental isomorphism between Quxiang Bilei and Bayesian inference. These five propositions are not mere mutual projections between two disparate conceptual frameworks, but genuinely novel insights emerging from their productive dialogue and critical engagement. This work provides a robust epistemological foundation for Bayesianism, offers rigorous mathematical support for the modern transformation of classical Chinese epistemology, and establishes a unified analytical framework for understanding the cognitive essence and fundamental limitations of artificial intelligence.

title: Finite-Capacity Prospect Theory: State-Dependent Risk Preferences under Resource, Attention, and Boundary-Risk Constraints
abstract: This paper develops a state-dependent version of Prospect Theory in which loss aversion, reference adaptation, and probability weighting vary with resource buffers, attention constraints, and boundary-risk context. Prospect Theory is usually treated as a descriptive account of deviations from expected-utility rationality. This paper proposes a finite-capacity bridge model: bounded agents operating under scarcity, limited attention, finite memory, and finite update capacity should exhibit state-dependent risk preferences. The model introduces a vector-valued resource buffer, perceived distance to a constraint threshold, event-class-dependent probability weighting, and a nudge-bandwidth model. It predicts that loss aversion should rise under scarcity and downside exposure, reference baselines should adapt more slowly under depletion, and probability weighting should become more distorted for low-capacity agents and boundary-relevant rare events. The paper includes deterministic synthetic simulations and a replication package, but does not fit human-subject data. It is intended as a theoretical working paper and empirical identification framework for future tests of state-dependent Prospect Theory.

title: Strategic Heterogeneity in Issue Positioning: Evidence from Congressional Campaigns
abstract: Do candidates maintain consistent left-right issue positions in congressional elections? How do district conditions influence variations in this consistency? While these questions are fundamental to the study of U.S. elections, surprisingly little is known about issue positioning in campaigns. We combine campaign platforms with machine learning methods to estimate the left-right orientations of U.S. House candidates (n = 4,509) across six salient issue areas in multiple election cycles (2018-2022). Our validated measures reveal meaningful within-candidate variation in issue positioning that systematically reflects district conditions. Candidates exhibit greater positional heterogeneity in districts that are not safe for their party and where constituent policy preferences are variable. We further demonstrate that this positional variation directly reflects district opinion, as issue-specific positioning aligns with constituency policy preferences. These findings reveal how candidate messaging varies systematically across issue domains and electoral contexts, fundamentally shaping the information environment that structures democratic choice and representation.

title: Securitized Symbolic Boundaries: Muslim Identity, Terrorism Discourse, and Public Opinion in Switzerland
authors: Anina Schwarzenbach, Livia Zöbeli, Emirhan Darcan
abstract: How do democratic societies embed religious difference within the logic of security governance? This article introduces the concept of securitized symbolic boundaries to analyze how religious and cultural distinctions become organized and sustained as matters of existential threat within public discourse. Building on symbolic boundary theory and securitization scholarship, we examine the discursive construction of Islam and Muslim identity within Swiss media coverage of extremism and terrorism between 1994 and 2024. Drawing on a corpus of 286,660 articles from 26 outlets, we combine topic modeling, Bayesian change-point detection, and interrupted time-series analysis to trace the structure, dynamics, and consequences of securitized boundary-making over three decades. We show that Muslim identity is disproportionately and distinctively linked to security frames relative to other religious identities within a corpus designed to capture extremist discourse across religious variants equally. This disproportionality is organized through three recurrent frames: religious governance, visible religious practices, and migration-related otherness. Crisis events—including major European terrorist attacks and domestic policy initiatives—function as critical junctures that reorganize rather than merely intensify this discourse, with the relative salience of frames shifting systematically across discursive phases. During such periods, ideologically diverse outlets temporarily converge on shared threat narratives, producing what we term crisis consensus: a narrowing of interpretive diversity that intensifies symbolic exclusion. Although these surges are episodic, elements of crisis framing persist, reshaping baseline discourse over time in ways consistent with long-term embedding rather than post-crisis equilibrium restoration. Linking these media patterns to repeated cross-sectional survey data, we find that periods of intensified securitized coverage align with more exclusionary public attitudes toward Muslims, with associations driven by overall coverage prominence rather than the content of specific frames. Together, the findings show how security discourse can transform religious difference into a durable symbolic boundary. The article identifies three mechanisms behind this process: boundary brightening during crises, temporary convergence across ideologically diverse outlets, and the persistence of crisis frames after acute events subside.

title: Quantum Coherence in Neural Microtubules: A Unified, Empirically Grounded, and Testable Framework for Gamma Oscillation Precision
abstract: Gamma-band oscillations (30-100 Hz) exhibit timing precision that challenges strictly classical accounts of neural synchronization. This manuscript presents a unified the- oretical framework proposing that quantum coherence in neural microtubules may serve as a modulatory mechanism to enhance gamma oscillation precision. We provide: (1) rigorous, step-by-step decoherence derivations constrained by thermal, electromagnetic, and mechanical channels; (2) empirically grounded parameters derived from microtubule electromagnetic oscillations and tryptophan superradiance experiments; (3) a conservative quantum-classical coupling mechanism that modulates pyramidal-interneuron network gamma (PING) and interneuron network gamma (ING) precision through weak electromagnetic fields (∼nT); (4) complete experimental designs integrating nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center quantum sensing with high-density electrophysiology; and (5) computational validation pipelines us- ing finite element modeling and stochastic simulations. We formalize the Perry Constant (κ ≈ 1.7±0.3 ms−1) as a proposed quantitative bridge linking coherence factor to precision enhancement. Our framework generates four primary testable predictions: measurable coherence-precision correlations (r >0.3), quantum-consistent temperature scaling (Tc ≈ 12 ± 3 K), resonance-selective electromagnetic eects (Q > 5 in 4060 Hz), and pharmacological selectivity for microtubule-targeting drugs. We transparently identify two major quantitative gaps in the initial framework: a ∼6 order-of-magnitude discrepancy between calculated eective coherence times (∼15 ns) and required functional timescales (∼ms), and an electric coupling energy (ΔV ≈ 3.2 μV) below ion channel threshold (∼4 mV). We then resolve these gaps through four physically justied mechanisms supported by novel simulations: (1) non-Markovian dynamics with structured spectral density extend coherence 10- 33× (to ∼165500 ns); (2) cooperative gating across ∼100 channels plus stochastic resonance amplication achieves ∼40% gating modulation; (3) lattice geometry simulation conrms collective decoherence scaling; and (4) phase-coherent accumulation over gamma cycles enables network-level detection (SNR ≈ 143). A revised PING network incorporating all mechanisms produces clear 40 Hz gamma locking (spike PLV = 0.74). Alternative classical explanations are systematically discussed and distinguished through quantum-specic signatures. This work represents a falsifiable, empirically testable contribution to quantum biology and neuroscience, avoiding speculative claims about consciousness generation while advancing our understanding of neural timing precision. Keywords: Quantum Coherence, Microtubules, Gamma Oscillations, Decoherence, Neural Precision, NV-Center Sensing, Quantum Biology, Perry Constant, Mathematical Modeling, Neuroscience

title: How do opinion-based and social group identities influence each other? Evidence from a survey experiment on class and transnational political issues
abstract: Do people identify with social groups because of their political opinions, or do they identify with political opinion camps because of their basis in social groups? We build a theoretical model of why opinion-based and social group-based polarization are connected and test this with a survey experiment embedded in the British Election Study Internet Panel. The opinion-based identities concern immigration and the environment, both strongly connected to the universalist-particularist cleavage. We social group identity focused on is the working class, which has considerable salience in the British context. When self-identifying working class respondents are informed that their class is the majority in an opinion-based group, they feel closer to that group. However, specific opinion-camps feel more distant from the working class when they believe this class is the majority in the opposing opinion-based group. Social group and opinion-based polarization are thus intertwined, one influencing the other.

title: Low health-information discernment as a public-health vulnerability in Europe: evidence from an eight-country survey
authors: Laurent Cordonier, Gérald Bronner, Florian Cafiero
abstract: Background: During health emergencies, people face an overabundance of information of varying reliability. Their responses to health-authority recommendations may depend on their general ability to discern accurate from inaccurate health claims. In this study, we examined the distribution and correlates of low health-information discernment in eight European countries and its links with acceptance of recommended health measures in a hypothetical pandemic scenario. Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional online survey of 24,203 adults in France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Italy, Poland, Spain, and Sweden. Respondents read a hypothetical pandemic scenario and reported their acceptance within that scenario of three recommended health measures: following preventive behaviours, taking a new antiviral treatment if infected, and receiving a new vaccine. Health-information discernment was measured as the difference between mean endorsement of accurate and inaccurate health claims unrelated to the pandemic scenario. Low discernment was defined as being in the lowest quartile of the pooled discernment distribution. We used country-specific raking weights and fitted adjusted regression models to estimate low-discernment prevalence, identify its correlates, and assess its associations with acceptance of the three recommended measures. We also estimated adjusted risk differences in low or uncertain acceptance for respondents with low health-information discernment versus all other respondents, and conducted country-stratified and sensitivity analyses. Findings: Low health-information discernment, defined as the lowest quartile of the pooled distribution, varied substantially across countries, from 18.2% in Sweden to 36.0% in Hungary. In adjusted analyses, low discernment was more likely among respondents with higher alternative therapy orientation (OR 1.76, 95% CI 1.66–1.86), higher trust in weak or non-institutional health sources (OR 1.70, 95% CI 1.60–1.81), and higher New Age orientation (OR 1.24, 95% CI 1.18–1.30). By contrast, it was less likely among respondents with higher trust in institutional health sources (OR 0.51, 95% CI 0.49–0.54) and higher trust in science and medicine (OR 0.69, 95% CI 0.66–0.73). Low health-information discernment was associated with lower acceptance of all three recommended measures in adjusted models using continuous acceptance scores: preventive behaviours (-0.32, 95% CI -0.35 to -0.30), antiviral treatment (-0.13, 95% CI -0.15 to -0.10), and vaccination (-0.13, 95% CI -0.16 to -0.10). In separate adjusted models using binary outcomes, low discernment was associated with a higher adjusted risk of low or uncertain acceptance of each measure. This risk was higher by 12.6 percentage points for preventive behaviours, 5.7 percentage points for antiviral treatment, and 5.1 percentage points for vaccination.

title: Between Perceived Fairness and Resignation: Examining Machine Heuristics, Privacy Cynicism and Self-Disclosure in Generative AI
abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) systems such as ChatGPT and DeepSeek require users to disclose nuanced prompts that may reveal highly sensitive personal information. Existing studies on privacy risks in generative AI are fragmented and rarely grounded in established privacy theories or comparative frameworks. To advance knowledge on generative AI privacy, a multi‑stage model of privacy decision‑making is proposed that integrates machine heuristics, privacy concerns, privacy cynicism and self‑disclosure. Drawing on two online surveys in China and the US (N = 1632), we examine how machine heuristic perceptions relate to privacy concerns, how concerns translate into privacy cynicism, and how cynicism is associated with self‑disclosure. Chinese respondents reported higher self‑disclosure, whereas US participants expressed greater privacy concerns, mistrust and uncertainty, but also higher self‑efficacy and use frequency. The findings suggest that perceptions of AI as fair, accurate, and having expertise are associated with lower privacy concerns, that concerns are positively linked to cynicism dimensions, and that cynicism exhibits divergent associations with self‑disclosure. These results are discussed and contextualized in relation to privacy scholarship and policy and design recommendation.

title: Alarms Without Smoke: Predicting and Addressing False Alarms in Urban Fire Services
abstract: False fire alarms waste essential resources and prevent fire services from responding to real fires in time. While previous research has mainly focused on improving hardware accuracy, the spatial and temporal patterns of false alarms remain largely unexplored. This study analyses 15 years of historical fire incident data from the West Midlands Fire Service, UK. The data reveal that attending false alarms accounts for 34% of total mobilisation time. Given this significant potential for resource savings, we applied machine learning to predict an alarm’s validity at the point of dispatch. Our proposed model achieves an accuracy of 86.0% and only misses 2.1% of real incidents. This study introduces a new geospatial perspective to mitigate the impact of false alarms. Furthermore, the model can be integrated into existing fire service policies to enhance public safety and urban resilience.

title: Foreign Exchange Intervention: Identification and Evidence
abstract: Foreign exchange intervention is widely used across low, middle and high income economies, yet its effectiveness remains disputed. The central difficulty is identification: interventions are typically endogenous to exchange rate movements, often bundled with other policies and sometimes unobserved. This survey reviews five decades of empirical research and reframes the debate as a problem of causal identification rather than a binary question of whether intervention works. The literature is organized by identification strategy: event studies, reaction-function residuals, controlled regressions, instrumental variables, high-frequency approaches, SVARs, structural portfolio-balance models and natural experiments. For each design, the source of identifying variation and the key assumptions required for causal interpretation are specified. Differences in reported effectiveness largely reflect differences in design, data and regime context. The overall finding is that intervention does affect exchange rates: evidence is strongest for short-run effects on returns and volatility, especially when operations are large, well-timed and implemented in thinner markets. Persistent exchange rate level effects are more sensitive to assumptions about asset substitutability, expectations and financial frictions. The survey provides guidance for empirical practice and highlights priorities for improved measurement and future research.

title: Assessing Foreign Interference During the "Year of Elections"
authors: Kevin T. Greene, Jeffrey J. Imbrogno, Jacob N Shapiro
abstract: How widespread was foreign interference in elections in 2024, the "global year of elections"? We compile new data on foreign election interference in each of the 63 national-level executive and legislative elections held in 2024. We find that foreign interference was common, there is clear evidence of it in 30 percent of elections and affecting 60 percent of the world’s voting population. Russia conducted the most interference efforts, targeting more elections than all other actors combined, and focusing largely on European elections. Interference was most often carried out through social media and by setting up fake news sites.

title: Data Resource Profile: Nursing and Midwifery Council Register Linked to Census 2021- England and Wales
authors: Michelle Jamieson, Nathan O'Connor, Caroline Kenny, Iain Maitland Atherton
abstract: Introduction: Nurses, midwives, and nursing associates form the largest group of registered health professionals in the United Kingdom. Research into the social circumstances of this workforce has been constrained by the absence of population-level data linking professional registration to sociodemographic characteristics. The NMC Register–Census 2021 linked dataset for England and Wales addresses this gap. Methods: The Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) provided five data extracts: cross-sectional extracts of the professional register as of March 2018, 2021, 2022, and 2023, and a leavers extract covering 2018–2021. A pseudonymised personal registration number links individuals across all five extracts. The Office for National Statistics (ONS) linked each extract to the 2021 England and Wales Census using deterministic and probabilistic record linkage methods. Results: The rebuilt 2021 register comprises 732,835 distinct registrants across all four UK nations. Of 614,975 registrants resident in England or Wales at census night and eligible for linkage, 565,680 (91.9%) were successfully linked to the Census. The linked dataset encompasses individual-level registration, sociodemographic, socioeconomic, household, migration, and geographic variables. Conclusion: The NMC-Census 2021 linked dataset for England and Wales is an ADR UK flagship resource enabling population-level analysis of the nursing and midwifery workforce and its social determinants. It supports research into workforce composition, retention, commuting geography, and longitudinal change. Annual additions of the NMC register data are planned. Accredited researchers may apply for access via the ONS Secure Research Service.

title: Belief and Resistance: Vulnerability to Authoritarian Disinformation in Turkey and the Effect of Counter-Strategies
authors: Aykut Öztürk, Seda Demiralp, Ugur Ozdemir, Candas Ayan
abstract: Contemporary authoritarian governments increasingly rely on disinformation as a tool of political control. While a growing literature examines the supply side of authoritarian disinformation, far less is known about how citizens engage with it at the individual level. We address this question, taking Turkey as a critical case. Drawing on an original survey fielded during the first two weeks of the disinformation campaign that followed the arrest of Istanbul Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu in March 2025, we move beyond the conventional focus on partisan attitudes to offer a more comprehensive account of individual-level vulnerability. We show that epistemic character traits, stable dispositions that structure how individuals relate to truth and knowledge, are consistent predictors of disinformation susceptibility, with effects that hold across partisan lines. A pre-registered survey experiment embedded in the same survey reveals that fact-checking modestly reduces belief in disinformation narratives, while a media literacy prime backfires, increasing endorsement of regime narratives. These findings advance our understanding of how authoritarian disinformation operates, who is most vulnerable to it, and what kinds of interventions are likely to help or harm, with implications for both the study of authoritarian politics and the design of counter-disinformation policy.

title: Manufacturing for Economic Growth, Industrial Relations 2026, and Comprehensive National Power: A Critical Assessment
abstract: This paper examines the Industrial Relations (Central) Rules, 2026, notified by the Ministry of Labour and Employment on 8 May 2026 through Gazette notification G.S.R. 342(E), and assesses their implications for manufacturing-led growth and Comprehensive National Power. The analysis situates the rules within the scholarly debate between Bhagwati and Panagariya on labour-intensive manufacturing, Rajan on domestic-oriented growth, and Rodrik on premature deindustrialization. Four provisions are documented: the 300-worker threshold for prior permission on lay-offs, retrenchments, and closures; the 14-day strike notice with a 60-day validity period applicable across all establishments; the 30 percent secret-ballot threshold for sole negotiating union recognition; and the Worker Re-skilling Fund under Rule 37. A comparative review of labour regimes in China, Vietnam, Bangladesh, Indonesia, the United States, the United Kingdom, and Germany indicates that India retains a prior-permission architecture for collective dismissals while peer jurisdictions operate on notice-and-consult models. The paper applies Bhonsle's framework on Comprehensive National Power to read the rules as a mechanism mediating economic, governance, social-harmony, and human-capital objectives, and identifies limits in coverage, digital access, and unemployment protection.

title: Global Credit Cycles and Pro-Business Rhetoric in Party Manifestos
authors: Timm Betz, Andrew Kerner, Cristina Bodea
abstract: We identify a novel pathway that links financial globalization to politics. We emphasize the effect of globalization on the relationships between governments and domestic business owners, who, like all borrowers, are subject to “Global Financial Cycles” originating from U.S. credit conditions. Downturns in these cycles, stemming from high U.S. interest rates, reduce credit availability, depress asset prices, and broadly worsen the outlook for private sector profits. While politicians have limited power to address the underlying financial conditions, they can adopt business-friendly politics to signal their willingness to compensate firms for the higher borrowing costs created from higher U.S. interest rates. We support our argument with evidence from party manifestos across 59 countries, covering 1963 to 2017. Our paper documents a new connection between global capital cycles and party positions during an era of largely unrestricted capital mobility.

title: УПРАВЛЕНИЕ КРЕДИТНЫМИ РИСКАМИ В БРОКЕРСКИХ КОМПАНИЯХ
abstract: Данная статья исследует современные подходы к эффективному управлению кредитными рисками в контексте работы брокерских компаний. Автор представляет систематический анализ методов и инструментов, необходимых для оценки и управления кредитными рисками, связанными с брокерской деятельностью. Особое внимание уделяется разработке моделей и стратегий минимизации рисков, а также внедрению современных технологий и аналитических методов. Статья содержит ценные практические рекомендации для брокерских компаний, стремящихся укрепить свою финансовую устойчивость и обеспечить безопасность клиентских средств.

title: Co-creating community-driven LGBTQ+ health and research priorities in Mongolia
authors: Dorjjantsan Ganbaatar, Thiago Melo Santos, Nicholas Hill, Cathy Vaughan, Enkhmaa Enkhbold, Erdeneburen Dorjpurev, Sugarmaa Myagmarjav, Meghan Bohren
abstract: LGBTQ+ research and policy in Mongolia largely focused on sexually transmitted infections (STIs), particularly among men who have sex with men (MSM). This narrow focus has resulted in a significant lack of evidence on the specific health needs, challenges, and priorities of diverse sub-communities beyond HIV/STIs. To address this, we collaborated with 19 local LGBTQ+ health and rights experts in a two-stage co-creation workshop to identify and prioritise key health issues and to develop a community-driven research agenda. Using thematic analysis, we identified 15 health priorities in Stage 1 and 31 research priorities in Stage 2. Findings from Stage 1 indicated that, despite variations in healthcare needs across sub-communities, participants consistently identified common systemic challenges. These included limited access to LGBTQ+-affirming healthcare services, pervasive prejudice and discrimination, and insufficient capacity within the healthcare system. In Stage 2, the identified research priorities were organised into six overarching domains to address existing gaps: community health needs, health sector capacity, emerging risks, quality of current services, current policy, and LGBTQ+ history and lived experiences. These community-driven health and research priorities can strengthen grassroots advocacy and inform future research, interventions, policy, and funding initiatives focused on LGBTQ+ health in Mongolia.

title: The Harder the State Hits: Coercive Intensity, Protest Repression, and Terrorist Backlash
abstract: Why does repression sometimes deter dissent but at other times generate terrorist backlash? Existing theories emphasise political opportunity, grievances, legitimacy and strategic interaction, but they do not fully explain when repression against dissent escalates into terrorist violence. I argue that backlash depends on the coercive mode through which state force is organised and interpreted. Coercion is more likely to deter when it remains proportionate, fair and predictable. Backlash becomes more likely when coercion creates two interconnected expectation gaps. The first is instrumental: compliance no longer appears sufficient to guarantee safety. The second is normative: the state violates its own claim to exercise force within bounded limits, signalling the exclusion of some groups rather than their protection. Under these conditions, excessive, arbitrary, indiscriminate or visibly disproportionate coercion weakens deterrence while intensifying grievance. It changes the moral ecology through which dissent is interpreted, making terrorist retaliation appear more strategically plausible or morally defensible. I test this argument using country-quarter event data from ACLED and GTD for 2018–2021. The results show that coercive military involvement in domestic security is associated with higher subsequent terrorist activity. The association follows a coercion-intensity gradient: excessive force and military protest repression are more strongly associated with subsequent terrorism than lower-intensity intervention or police-led repression. These findings hold across alternative controls, temporal diagnostics and balancing checks, but remain observational. They suggest that repression backfires not simply because it is forceful, but because some forms of coercion undermine the legitimacy and predictability on which deterrence depends.

title: Collections grow by volume but diversify by exception: donor biographies in MoMA's gift channel, 1929-2024
abstract: A museum's permanent collection is publicly narrated in the first person plural, as choices the institution made. In its gift channel, though, the practical actor is often a single donor handing over a coordinated block of works at once. We read nearly a century of MoMA's donor-identified gift records (1929--2024) as a sequence of donor biographies and ask what each named donor actually changed about whom the collection represents. The recurring surprise is that volume and influence come apart. The donors who give the most works tend to leave the collection's gender and geographic mix where they found it, while the donors who move that mix do so through rare single-year mass gifts rather than a lifetime of collecting. From the six biographies we read most closely we draw a small vocabulary of donor logics, offered as a tool museums can use to read their own gift channels.

title: A Systematic Review of the Effects of Binge Drinking on University Life: Academic, Social, and Behavioral Implications
abstract: Binge drinking is a major public health issue widely associated with university life, particularly during the transition to independent living. High-intensity alcohol consumption among university students is linked to serious physiological, psychological, academic, and social consequences, including neurodegeneration, sleep disturbances, emotional distress, impaired attendance, and risky behaviors. However, existing research remains fragmented across academic, social, and behavioral domains, limiting a holistic understanding of its impact on university life. Additionally, the field relies heavily on cross-sectional studies and self-reported data, making it difficult to establish causal relationships between psychological distress and alcohol misuse. This systematic review aims to analyze the effects of binge drinking on university life by examining its influence on academic performance, social interactions, and maladaptive behavioral patterns. Following the PRISMA protocol, peer-reviewed articles published between 2020 and 2026 from databases such as Scopus and Sage Journals were reviewed. Ten contemporary studies and foundational literature were synthesized. Findings support the Self-Medication Hypothesis, showing that students often use alcohol to cope with ADHD symptoms, loneliness, and emotional distress. The review also identified academic impairment perceptions, changing gender patterns, and the need for longitudinal research and evidence-based university interventions.

title: Short-term rental accommodation: models, impacts and policy responses
authors: Nicole Gurran, Caitlin Buckle, Laurence Troy, Zahra Nasreen, Laura Crommelin, Peter Phibbs, Pranita Shrestha, Declan Redmond, Jason Harrison, Megan Robinson
abstract: This research investigates a range of short-term rental accommodation (STRA) models across Australia and their impacts on housing markets and communities. It identifies the policy responses most likely to resolve the competing demands for short- and long-term rental housing. Many communities are concerned about the housing market and neighbourhood impacts of STRA. Platforms like Airbnb have increased demand for residential property and enabled owners to finance housing investment in new ways. Understanding the range of STRA models, provider motivations, and impacts in different metropolitan and regional settings is key to effective policy development and regulation.

title: Criterios para el uso transparente, ético y responsable de la inteligencia artificial en publicaciones científicas (CRITERIA)
authors: Lluís Codina, Pere Freixa, Simón Peña-Fernández, carlos Lopezosa
abstract: CRITERIA propone un marco de referencia para orientar a las revistas científicas en la elaboración de políticas sobre el uso transparente, ético y responsable de la inteligencia artificial en la investigación y en la publicación académica. El documento parte de una premisa doble: por un lado, la IA puede aportar eficiencia y apoyo en tareas de ideación, diseño, análisis, síntesis y mejora textual; por otro, puede introducir riesgos graves para la integridad científica, entre ellos plagio, referencias inventadas, datos fabricados, errores factuales, sesgos y erosión del pensamiento crítico. La propuesta se articula en torno a cinco puntos cardinales: la IA no puede ser acreditada como autora, la responsabilidad recae íntegramente en las personas autoras, todo uso de IA exige supervisión crítica humana, el manuscrito debe conservar la voz y la unidad de estilo del autor, y los usos sustanciales deben declararse con transparencia y trazabilidad. Sobre esta base, CRITERIA distingue entre usos permitidos con declaración obligatoria, usos instrumentales no declarables y usos no permitidos, subrayando que la declaración no legitima prácticas contrarias a la integridad académica. Además, ofrece criterios operativos para declarar usos sustanciales, aplicar un test de sustancialidad y evitar la fatiga y el estigma de declaración.

title: Beyond Description: Causal Inference for Life-Course Trajectories Using Sequence Analysis and G-Computation
abstract: Life-course research asks how employment and family trajectories shape long-run outcomes, yet most empirical studies rely on a cluster-then-regress strategy that cannot recover causal effects when treatment and covariates evolve jointly over time. I propose a two-stage framework that integrates sequence analysis with g-computation. In the first stage, multi-channel sequence analysis and clustering identify empirically prevalent trajectory types whose medoids serve as counterfactual interventions for which positivity is empirically defensible. In the second stage, the causal effect of each medoid trajectory is estimated via g-computation with forward simulation of time-varying covariates using SuperLearner. A semi-synthetic simulation calibrated to the NLSY79 reveals a clear estimator hierarchy: cluster-adjusted regression recovers only a small fraction of the true causal effect, while flexible g-computation recovers the true effects most accurately. An empirical application to NLSY79 data reveals substantial wealth effects of sustained full-time employment and stable family formation. The framework offers life-course researchers a practical approach for defining trajectory interventions and estimating their causal effects.

title: “Just to keep my thoughts good”: Emotion management and ideological reinforcement among mothers on Facebook
abstract: While the psychological and emotional impacts of “intensive mothering” are well documented, we know less about the technological strategies contemporary mothers use to cope with these anxieties as they try to “do it right”. Drawing on qualitative data collected through 18 months of digital ethnographic research, including observation and interviews with 29 mothers, I argue that mothers engage in digitally-mediated emotion management by strategically using Facebook to alleviate their anxieties. In joining groups that make them feel good and leaving groups that make them feel bad, mothers “orchestrate” their Facebook experience in order to maximize positive feelings. I argue that this strategy brings mothers’ emotions into alignment with cultural expectations about how mothering "ought" to feel, while also contributing to ideological reinforcement around what it means to mother “correctly”.

title: From Sector to Access Ecosystem: A Seven-Layer Frame for Online-Only Research on the Digital Class
abstract: This article proposes a seven-layer organizational frame for online-only research on the digital class in state-entangled digital states — contexts in which conventional methods (interviews, representative surveys, labor-market statistics) are either unavailable or systematically biased, and where the sectoral optics of “IT firms plus workers” cannot hold the simultaneous weight of state, regulatory, investment, educational, symbolic-mediation, and civic-audit layers. Conceptually, the article proposes a shift from “sector” to “access-conversion ecosystem”: technical capital is converted into product, infrastructural, financial, educational, symbolic, and accountability resources through seven conversion functions (L1–L7). Methodologically, it proposes an online-only sampling frame with an evidence-ladder discipline (SRC/TRC/EVD/LNK/SNP/AMB registers and EV_STRONG/CONF_3 grades) and an explicitly tagged bias-correction supplement that turns visibility bias from a methodological limitation into a coded variable and a diagnostic instrument for the sociology of online visibility. The Kazakhstan case (Pilot 1: 150 core organizations across the seven layers, with 30 bias-correction supplement candidates) reveals four structural observations: the production core is less directly observable than its public periphery; gatekeeping (L4) is asymmetrically centralized; the education pipeline (L5) operates as a promise machine with a gap between the visibility of promise and the visibility of outcome; symbolic mediation (L6) and civic audit (L7) function as independent capitals, irreducible to “industry plus context”. The “visibility ≠ influence” discipline emerges not as a methodological caveat but as substantive defense of mapped actors against the forms of political miscodification typical of state-entangled contexts. The instrument is applicable to parallel digital states with active ecosystem policy and a developed public-visibility infrastructure.

title: Natural resource curse versus Dutch disease: the explanatory primacy of New Developmentalism over the paradox of plenty
abstract: This article reevaluates the two main explanatory approaches to the paradox of plenty: natural resource curse and Dutch disease. It is argued that the neo-institutionalism literature, while relevant for identifying political pathologies and rent capture incentives, operates predominantly at the level of effects rather than causes. The neoclassical model of Dutch disease, in turn, is limited to temporary price shocks. The article contends that Dutch disease, reformulated by New Developmentalism as an endogenous and chronic market coordination failure − anchored in Ricardian rents and multiple exchange rate equilibria − constitutes the structuring mechanism. The article proposes and formalizes a typology of models within the natural resource curse and Dutch disease theories.

title: A INTERPRETAÇÃO DO DESENVOLVIMENTO DEPENDENTE E ASSOCIADO DE FERNANDO HENRIQUE CARDOSO: UMA REVISITAÇÃO CRÍTICA
abstract: O artigo tem como escopo de revisitar a interpretação sociológica do desenvolvimento dependente e associado da América Latina para Fernando Henrique Cardoso. A hipótese elencada pela pesquisa é de que Cardoso faz uma leitura equivocada sobre desenvolvimento dependente e associado via poupança externa, crendo que o transbordamento de capital forte oriundo das economias centrais seria capaz de alavancar a sofisticação da estrutura produtiva brasileira. O artigo empregou como procedimentos metodológico revisão bibliográfica do autor pesquisado, de renomados pesquisadores que se dedicam a temática e dados empíricos da realidade econômica contemporânea do Brasil. O texto foi dividido em duas seções, além da introdução e considerações finais. Na primeira seção, apresentam-se os principais preceitos da interpretação sociológica da dependência e desenvolvimento de Cardoso. Na segunda seção, demonstra-se como que os preceitos de desenvolvimento dependente e associado de Cardoso levaram a estrutura produtiva brasileira a desnacionalização, a desindustrialização e a reprimarização. O resultado que o texto chega é de que embora a interpretação da dependência e desenvolvimento de Cardoso seja uma abordagem original e intricada, o autor falha ao empregar uma metodologia hipotética e dedutiva, menosprezando aspectos concretos e indutivos da relação econômica centro-periferia, presumindo que seria possível o desenvolvimento econômico dos países periféricos via poupança externa.

title: Revisitando o conceito de Desenvolvimento Econômico e a Escola de Pensamento do Desenvolvimentismo na Economia
abstract: O presente trabalho tem como objetivo revisitar e sistematizar os conceitos de Desenvolvimento Econômico e o de Desenvolvimentismo. A hipótese levantada pela pesquisa é a de que em razão da processualidade histórico-social das Ciências Econômicas, os termos Desenvolvimento Econômico e Desenvolvimentismo podem ser categorizados de forma distinta por opção epistemológica e didática na atualidade. O artigo optou por realizar um estudo bibliográfico. Os procedimentos metodológicos para selecionar as referências foram pesquisa bibliográfica focada nos autores com análise histórica, concreta e indutiva da economia e do fenômeno de desenvolvimento econômico. Os resultados obtidos são de que os termos Desenvolvimento Econômico e Desenvolvimentismo podem ser categorizados de forma distinta, o primeiro como conceito empregado por diversas escolas do pensamento econômico; o segundo é uma Escola de Pensamento Econômico em si e que possui quatro correntes teóricas (Desenvolvimentismo Clássico, Social Desenvolvimentismo, Novo Desenvolvimentismo, Desenvolvimentismo Socialista de Mercado).

title: Cultura do privilégio: entrave para o desenvolvimento econômico na América Latina
abstract: O presente artigo tem como objetivo contribuir para o entendimento dos entraves para um projeto de desenvolvimento econômico na América Latina contemporânea. A premissa elencada pela pesquisa é a de que os projetos e as estratégias desenvolvimentistas executados pelos governos vanguardistas nos países da América Latina sofrem de entraves e se tornam truncados em virtude, frequentemente, da cultura do privilégio presente na sociedade latino-americana. O artigo optou em realizar pesquisa de revisão bibliográfica pautada em leitura sintópica. Os resultados obtidos são de que, na América Latina, grande parte da elite dirigente carrega introjetada um desprezo pelo seu próprio povo –visto como “subgente”– e um fascínio e subalternidade perante o colonizador –economias centrais– visto como modelos de humanos e de civilização. Isso faz com que haja uma cultura do privilégio responsável por um horror social e fator relevante pelo entrave ao desenvolvimento econômico na região.

title: COOPERAÇÃO BRASIL – ÁFRICA EM BIOCOMBUSTÍVEIS DURANTE O GOVERNO LULA: UMA PARCERIA PARA O DESENVOLVIMENTO
authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Beatriz Rodrigues Bessa Mattos
abstract: O artigo propõe realizar uma análise sobre a cooperação no setor de biocombustíveis entre Brasil e África durante o governo do Presidente Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva (2003 – 2010). O estudo procurou, ademais, elencar os principais projetos, tratados e memorandos assinados pelo Brasil, visando à promoção dos combustíveis renováveis na África.

title: CHINA-AFRICA RELATIONS IN POLITICAL ECONOMY OF THE WORLD-SYSTEM: IN BETWEEN EXCLUDING-INSERTION AND INCLUDING-INSERTION
authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Mônica Heinzelmann Portella de Aguiar
abstract: The purpose of this article is to investigate how the current China-Africa relations integrate the Political Economy of the World-System. The paper starts by explaining the theoretical framework of the research, the World-systems theory. Upon examination of the current economic and trade relationship between China and Africa, the research assessed two different models that the underdeveloped economies integrate the international economy: the excluding-insertion and the including-insertion. The article used the descriptive-qualitative methodology, using primary and secondary sources. The results indicate that China, when dealing with Africa, is reproducing exploitative power behaviour, the same way all nations are or have been part of the "leader circle" of the world-system have done previously. Keywords: Political Economic of the World-System; Economic development; International Political Economy; Chinese Foreign Policy; Interstate relations China-Africa.

title: When Cumulative Advantage Inverts: Within-Canon Stratification in Korean Modern Art, 1929-2002
abstract: In the first decade of an artist's career, dense ties to a trusted circle of galleries and juries protect against stagnation. After a decade, the same ties begin to constrain, and the cost intensifies with cumulative recognition. The cumulative-advantage tradition predicts the opposite: that within an already-consecrated elite, the most-endorsed members should be the best insulated. This paper documents the regularity that runs the other way. Among 495 modern Korean artists assembled by the DA-Arts oral-history project in 2000, a canon spanning 1929 to 2002 and 16,424 institutional encounters, the protective value of dense ties is non-stationary across career time. The least-validated members of the canon show no penalty for repeated engagement with the same institutional partners; the most-validated face the steepest. This is a within-canon Matthew-inversion, not a field-level reversal of cumulative advantage. Because the within-canon recognition axis is built from the same evaluative apparatus the analysis treats as the outcome, the gradient documents how a consecration apparatus distributes its own outputs at the top of its own scale, not an independent recognition signal. The paper develops one candidate interpretation, evaluative redundancy, the diminishing informational return of repeated endorsement from the same gatekeeper. A ritualization reading is observationally equivalent on these data, and the paper leaves it empirically open. Whether the same within-canon Matthew-inversion recurs in other consecrated populations, from the Hollywood A-list to elite journalists, is the natural next test.

title: O PENSAMENTO DA CEPAL DE 2010 A 2018: O ENFOQUE NA MUDANÇA ESTRUTURAL PRODUTIVA PARA IGUALDADE (MEPI)
authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Hermano Caixeta Ibrahim, Leandro Pereira Morais
abstract: O artigo tem como unidade de análise a Comissão Econômica para América Latina e Caribe das Nações Unidas (CEPAL/ONU). O objetivo é de investigar a atual fase de seu pensamento com forte enfoque na igualdade. A pesquisa categoriza o pensamento cepalino em duas fases distintas: a primeira, do Estado-empreendedor e método histórico-estruturalista (1948-1990), e a segunda, do Estado-gerencial e método histórico-neoestruturalista (1990-atualidade). A hipótese elencada é de que o pensamento cepalino desde 2010 se afastou da proposta de Transformação Produtiva com Equidade (TPE) – defendido, inicialmente, pelo Neoestruturalismo – e está estabelecendo uma nova concepção: Mudança Estrutural Produtiva para Igualdade (MEPI) A pesquisa optou em priorizar como procedimentos técnicos uma abordagem sistemática por meio da avaliação crítica dos dados bibliográficos e documentais sobre a temática, sobretudo os próprios documentos produzidos pela CEPAL.

title: A importância da Economia Social e Solidária (ESS) para as propostas da Cepal na atualidade
authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Leandro Pereira Morais, Miguel Juan Bacic, Daniel Francisco Nagao Menezes
abstract: O artigo correlaciona os estudos e os autores da Economia Solidária e Social com a nova agenda de pesquisa da Cepal, estabelecendo pontos de contato, especialmente, com a Agenda Regional de Desenvolvimento Social Inclusivo e com a Mudança Estrutural Produtiva para Igualdade na América Latina. Os procedimentos técnico-metodológico foram: abordagem sistemática por meio da avaliação dos dados bibliográficos e documentais da Cepal e o campo acadêmico da ESS. Os resultados revelaram que a Cepal teria capacidade técnica, humana e material para contribuir com os avanços dos estudos da ESS, quantificando, qualificando e potencializando uma eficiência social no desenvolvimento econômico latino-americano.

title: Capitalismo agrário e movimentos campesinos no Paraguai
authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Fernando Antonio da Costa Vieira
abstract: O artigo busca analisar, por meio de levantamento bibliográfico e documental, o desenvolvimento do capitalismo rural e os movimentos sociais campesinos no Paraguai. Primeiramente, a pesquisa procura abordar a matriz empresarial-monopolista-agroexportadora de ocupação e de exploração da terra no Paraguai, com a significativa influência de ruralistas brasileiros, em detrimento da matriz familiar-cooperativista-alimentícia. Na segunda parte, empenha-se em apresentar os principais movimentos sociais campesinos do país, seus embates e meios de atuação. Por fim, demonstra a importância destes últimos para a democratização da terra, a soberania e a segurança alimentar paraguaia.

title: Relações Brasil-China: uma parceria para o desenvolvimento ou uma relação de codependência?
authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Mônica Heinzelmann Portella de Aguiar, Carlos Eduardo Tauil
abstract: Desde 2000, as relações sino-brasileiras vêm se aprofundando ao ponto da China ter se tornado o maior parceiro comercial do Brasil. Este artigo visa analisar a progressão destas relações econômicas e políticas a fim de compreender qual o impacto que a ascensão recente da China no contexto internacional teve sobre o desenvolvimento brasileiro. Dentro do referencial da Teoria da Dependência, a pesquisa levanta a pregunta se a parceria com a China contribuiu para o efetivo desenvolvimento brasileiro ou se foi criada uma nova forma de dependência. A primeira parte da pesquisa aborda a Teoria da Dependência e sua interpretação das relações entre países periféricos e centrais no complexo sistema internacional. A segunda parte apresenta um breve histórico das relações sino-brasileiras e analisa o seu atual estágio. A pesquisa propõe um novo conceito para interpretar as relações entre esses dois países semiperiféricos: o de codependência. Como procedimento técnico-metodológicos, a pesquisa utilizou de fontes primárias e secundárias, como indicadores econômicos, produção bibliográfica e documental.

title: CRISE DEMOCRÁTICA E AS PROPOSIÇÕES DO MUNICIPALISMO LIBERTÁRIO DE MURRAY BOOKCHIN E DO RADICALISMO DEMOCRÁTICO DE CHANTAL MOUFFE
abstract: O presente trabalho tem como objetivo apresentar e comparar os escritos de Murray Bookchin e sua Teoria de Municipalismo Libertário e os de Chantal Mouffe com seu Radicalismo Democrático. Ambos, pouco estudados no Brasil, têm seus escritos marcados pela busca de uma democracia mais radical e plural. A hipótese que o texto levanta é a de que enquanto o pensamento de Bookchin visa não somente a radicalização da democracia, mas a adoção do próprio método democrático em sua forma mais pura como mecanismo de superação do sistema capitalista; as prescrições de Mouffe se mantêm reformista e de certo modo flertando com o liberalismo, a despeito de utilizar um campo semântico e conceitual socialista. A apresentação dos autores e de seus pensamentos respeitou a ordem cronológica na qual cada um produziu seus trabalhos, Bookchin na década de 1960 e Mouffe na década de 1990. Assim, na primeira parte dedicou-se a explanar sobre o Municipalismo Libertário, na segunda parte sobre o Radicalismo Democrático e nas considerações finais o trabalho intentou em fazer um balanço entre as duas teorias.

title: Análise do Discurso e o uso dos meios de comunicação na Política Externa de Boa Vizinhança
abstract: O presente artigo, fazendo uso da Análise do Discurso, buscou lançar um olhar na utilização e nos impactos causados pelos meios de comunicação, no Brasil, na Política Externa de Boa Vizinhança do governo de Franklin Delano Roosevelt, presidente dos Estados Unidos da América – EUA de 1933 a 1945. Primeiramente, o estudo procurou traçar um panorama sobre os preceitos essenciais da Análise do Discurso, valendo-se, mormente, dos estudos de Michel Foucault. Posteriormente, foi realizado um breve levantamento bibliográfico e histórico, de fontes primárias e secundárias, com vistas a apresentar a produção discursiva da Política Externa de Boa Vizinhança e suas principais diretrizes para a América Latina, sobretudo para o Brasil. O trabalho inferiu, por fim, que os aspectos culturais produzidos pela Política Externa de Boa Vizinhança e vendidos aos demais países latino-americanos divulgaram estilos de vida, conceitos, visões e foi uma das primeiras exportações de versões idealizadas do American way of Life.

title: A macroeconomia do Social Desenvolvimentismo: um estudo da Rede Desenvolvimentista
abstract: O presente artigo tem como unidade de análise o Social Desenvolvimentismo. A categoria de análise é sua macroeconomia. O estudo adotou como procedimento metodológico a abordagem sistemática por meio da seleção, do fichamento e da avaliação crítica de dados bibliográficos que formam o arcabouço teórico do Social Desenvolvimentismo, sobretudo os estudos da Rede Desenvolvimentista (Rede D). O artigo foi dividido em duas seções; a primeira apresenta uma breve história da edificação da escola e da operacionalização conceitual do Social Desenvolvimentismo; a segunda seção concentra-se em elucidar a macroeconomia do Social Desenvolvimento. Os resultados da pesquisa são que o Social Desenvolvimentismo resgata o caráter político na macroeconomia ao defender um modelo de política e de regime macroeconômico mais adaptável e menos engessado, no entanto sem enveredar para “aventuras” ou “populismos”. O modelo, assim, contribui para o amadurecimento do debate desenvolvimentista no Brasil atualmente.

title: A IMIGRAÇÃO HAITIANA PARA O BRASIL: CAUSAS E DESAFIOS
authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Carlos Alberto Alencar de Andrade, Beatriz Rodrigues Bessa Mattos
abstract: O artigo procura analisar a recente imigração haitiana para o Brasil. O texto utiliza-se de conceitos como visto humanitário e refugio. Embasa-se em autores da Teoria Histórico-Estruturalista para compreender os fatores de atração e repulsão presente nos fluxos migratórios. O estudo, por fim, busca averiguar as ações e a estrutura político-administrativa brasileira para lidar com os imigrantes.

title: A Reproductive Health and Mortality Gradient for Women, Mothers, and Girls in Non-Kin Out-of-Home Placement Evidence, Mechanism, and the Federal Disability Framework
abstract: Background. Out-of-home care (OOHC), defined as the removal of a child from a parent into kinship, non-kin foster, or congregate placement, is one of the largest non-medical interventions applied to women and girls over the life course. The evidence base for the general adult-mortality effect of OOHC is now mature. A six-country meta-analysis covering 3.2 million individuals reports a pooled all-cause adult mortality hazard ratio of 2.21 (95% CI 1.62 to 3.02), with tripled suicide risk. The sexual-health literature has not yet developed the female-specific reproductive-health arc that runs through this mortality signal, nor the parallel mortality burden carried by mothers whose children are taken into care. Objectives. The paper has three. The first is to synthesise the strongest causal-design evidence linking OOHC exposure as a child, and child removal as a mother, to female-specific reproductive-health endpoints, namely accelerated pubertal timing, sexual victimisation and exploitation in placement, sexually transmitted infection (STI) and HIV burden, adolescent pregnancy, obstetric morbidity, and maternal mortality. The second is to locate this evidence within the developmental and HPA-axis biological framework that links childhood adversity to reproductive harm. The third is to place the harm within the federal disability framework that already designates reproductive function as a protected major bodily function under the Americans with Disabilities Act Amendments Act of 2008 and HHS Section 504. Findings. Multiple convergent lines of evidence document a graded female-specific reproductive-health and mortality burden. The lines include the instrumental-variable design (Doyle 2007), two discordant-sibling register cohorts (Brännström 2020; Wall-Wieler 2018), a 50-year longitudinal cohort (Sacker 2021), four meta-analytic syntheses (Batty 2022; Hughes 2017; Mendle 2019; Zhang 2023), and two large quasi-experimental administrative cohorts from Manitoba (Brownell 2024; Kenny 2026). Girls in OOHC experience earlier pubertal onset, four-fold elevated lifetime sexual-abuse risk relative to non-OOHC peers, two- to three-fold higher rates of adolescent pregnancy, elevated laboratory-confirmed STI prevalence, and disproportionate representation among commercial sexual exploitation cases. Manitoba register evidence demonstrates that mothers whose children are removed by child protective services face all-cause mortality 3 to 4 times above peers, with deaths concentrated in suicide, overdose, and homicide. The biological mechanism is consistent across studies and includes dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal and hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axes, chronic inflammation, prenatal cortisol programming, and 11β-HSD2 suppression.

title: От IT-сектора к экосистеме доступа: семислойная рамка online-only исследования цифрового класса
