abstract: This article proposes a seven-layer organizational frame for online-only research on the digital class in state-entangled digital states — contexts in which conventional methods (interviews, representative surveys, labor-market statistics) are either unavailable or systematically biased, and where the sectoral optics of “IT firms plus workers” cannot hold the simultaneous weight of state, regulatory, investment, educational, symbolic-mediation, and civic-audit layers. Conceptually, the article proposes a shift from “sector” to “access-conversion ecosystem”: technical capital is converted into product, infrastructural, financial, educational, symbolic, and accountability resources through seven conversion functions (L1–L7). Methodologically, it proposes an online-only sampling frame with an evidence-ladder discipline (SRC/TRC/EVD/LNK/SNP/AMB registers and EV_STRONG/CONF_3 grades) and an explicitly tagged bias-correction supplement that turns visibility bias from a methodological limitation into a coded variable and a diagnostic instrument for the sociology of online visibility. The Kazakhstan case (Pilot 1: 150 core organizations across the seven layers, with 30 bias-correction supplement candidates) reveals four structural observations: the production core is less directly observable than its public periphery; gatekeeping (L4) is asymmetrically centralized; the education pipeline (L5) operates as a promise machine with a gap between the visibility of promise and the visibility of outcome; symbolic mediation (L6) and civic audit (L7) function as independent capitals, irreducible to “industry plus context”. The “visibility ≠ influence” discipline emerges not as a methodological caveat but as substantive defense of mapped actors against the forms of political miscodification typical of state-entangled contexts. The instrument is applicable to parallel digital states with active ecosystem policy and a developed public-visibility infrastructure.

title: Digital Diffusion: Mobile Internet and the Spread of Cultural Scripts about Family Life
authors: Casey F. Breen, Ridhi Kashyap, Till Koebe
abstract: Global cultural diffusion theories have long emphasized how institutional actors such as intergovernmental organizations, international non-governmental organizations, and state agencies spread cultural scripts. We theorize that mobile internet constitutes a qualitatively distinct pathway for the diffusion of these scripts, shifting exposure from institutionally mediated and episodic to more individualized, continuous, and algorithmically curated. This reconfiguration changes both which cultural models individuals encounter and the frequency with which individuals are exposed to them. Using the staggered rollout of mobile internet across Nigeria as an empirical case, we find that expanding coverage reduces fertility, corresponding to a 9% relative decline in the annual probability of birth, consistent with shifts in contraceptive use, ideal family size, and women’s autonomy in financial and healthcare decision-making. These findings suggest that digital infrastructure does not simply extend existing diffusion processes but can reshape them, with measurable consequences for family life.

title: A Multi-District Markov Chain Monte Carlo Sampler for Redistricting
abstract: Random sampling of redistricting plans has become a standard tool for detecting gerrymandering and evaluating proposed maps. Most MCMC redistricting algorithms operate by merging two adjacent districts and splitting them back into two new districts at each step of the chain. I extend this Merge-Split framework with the Multiple Merge Sequential Split (MMSS) algorithm, which generalizes each step to merge ℓ ≥ 2 adjacent districts and repartition them into ℓ new districts via a sequence of ℓ − 1 uniform spanning tree cuts. MMSS targets the same spanning-forest-weighted distribution used by existing samplers, and I derive the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio. I validate the algorithm by comparing samples against exactly enumerated distributions on small grid maps and compare its scalability with two related algorithms on a realistic redistricting problem. The algorithm supports any value of ℓ ≥ 2, giving users direct control over per-step exploration. MMSS is implemented in the open-source redist R package.

title: Limits of Language Models for Qualitative Coding: Detecting Calls to Action in r/Antiwork
abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated that large language models (LLMs) can match human experts’ accuracy in qualitatively coding social media data. However, proprietary LLMs accessed through APIs raise concerns related to cost, accessibility, and data protection. Although smaller language models (SLMs) have received less scholarly attention, they can be deployed locally and are open-access, which in turn can mitigate many of the ethical concerns of LLMs. This study evaluates three open-access SLMs in two zero-shot qualitative coding tasks identifying and classifying calls to action (CTAs) within posts from the Reddit community r/Antiwork. Drawing on framing theory and the concept of infrapolitics, we examine how models interpret both collective and individually oriented forms of mobilizing discourse. Across both tasks, models demonstrated consistently low agreement with human-coded ground truth and were least successful at identifying implicit, individual, and infrapolitical calls to action embedded in personal narrative. Qualitative close reading revealed that models most accurately identified CTAs when posts contained explicit directive language and recognizable collective action framing, but were less able to identify CTAs embedded in community-specific and context-dependent forms of mobilization. These findings suggest that zero-shot prompting may be poorly suited to qualitative coding tasks involving implicit social movement discourse. Furthermore, computational approaches to researching mobilization may systematically privilege explicit collective action over individualized or infrapolitical forms of resistance.

title: Reframing biodiversity conservation using social data and artificial intelligence
authors: Oscar Morton, Sebastian Heilpern, Melissa Arias, Sharon Baruch-Mordo, Lucy Cash, Nathan Fox, Annette Hübschle, Koffi Valentin Mawougnigan, Ting Luo, Jacqueline Jürgens
abstract: Human behaviour is central to biodiversity loss, yet conservation rarely harnesses the wealth of social datasets that capture and reflect these behaviours. Here, we argue that integrating diverse social data with recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) can reveal early behavioural signals of emerging threats, enabling a shift from reactive mitigation to anticipatory action. Using illegal wildlife trade mitigation as an illustrative example, we outline how AI-enabled analyses of social data can be applied across the conservation project lifecycle, and highlight key safeguards and priority steps for piloting these approaches in policy-relevant settings.

title: On the New Design Approach for Mountain Villas
abstract: Based on the value of mountain villas and the problems they face, this paper proposes a new design approach for mountain villas using electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft as the primary means of transportation. The advantages of this new type of mountain villa are: 1) No need to construct roads; they can be built on any terrain, even on isolated peaks, allowing residents to stay far away from bustling cities without inconvenience. 2) The scenery and privacy will be of the highest order, offering residents a breathtaking living experience and ultimate enjoyment. These villas will set a new benchmark for luxury estates and are also suitable for high end vacation and leisure development. 3) The environmental impact is within acceptable limits, and at the same time, these villas will add new landscape features to mountainous areas. However, there are also some non negligible issues, such as construction costs, eVTOL traffic safety, seismic and wind resistance of the buildings, and public perception. These issues are discussed in detail.

title: Hans Hass and Jacques-Yves Cousteau. Pioneering Achievements and Structural Inequalities in the Construction of Maritime Memory.
abstract: This study examines the parallel yet highly divergent careers of Hans Hass and Jacques-Yves Cousteau, two central pioneers of twentieth century underwater exploration. While both contributed substantially to marine science, diving technology, and the visual mediation of the underwater world, their long-term global recognition developed in markedly different ways. Drawing on diverse interdisciplinary perspectives, the article explores how geopolitical conditions, institutional support systems, narrative self-presentation, and the rise of a global media ecology shaped their public visibility. Beyond a biographical or technical comparison, the juxtaposition of Hass and Cousteau is deliberately employed as a conceptual case study to examine the formation of maritime memory in the twentieth century underwater research. The findings demonstrate that Cousteau’s worldwide prominence resulted not solely from scientific or technological achievement, but from his exceptional integration into powerful institutional, technological, and cultural infrastructures that amplified and internationalized his work. In contrast, Hass—despite significant early innovation and ecological awareness—was constrained by weaker institutional embeddedness, a scientifically restrained and emotionally neutral serial television aesthetic with limited long-term repeatability, and subsequent theoretical isolation. A decisive factor in this divergence was Cousteau’s entrepreneurial engagement with emerging television markets, which transformed underwater exploration into a scalable media enterprise and provided the structural basis for his enduring visibility. Overall, the analysis suggests that maritime memory is shaped less by scientific merit alone than by the ways media, politics, and economic scalability stabilize certain cultural narratives. These dynamics have produced asymmetrical legacies that continue to shape how ocean exploration is remembered and imagined today.

title: Locally-Driven Developmental United Front Work: County-Level Mobilization in China's Rural Revitalization
abstract: This article develops the concept of "locally-driven developmental united front work" to explain how county-level united front departments in China have moved beyond traditional political coordination and elite co-optation toward active involvement in economic growth, cultural tourism, and social governance. Drawing on participant observation and archival analysis of Shangyou County (Jiangxi Province) between 2022 and 2025, the study identifies four mechanisms through which united front resources are transformed into developmental outcomes: resource capitalization, platform repurposing, institutional bricolage, and cross-sector brokerage. The analysis focuses on five empirical domains: cultural tourism integration, private-sector innovation, non-Party cadre cultivation, grassroots organizational innovation, and digital capacity building. The findings suggest that under conditions of fiscal constraint and strong upper-level policy support, county-level united front leaders can function as developmental entrepreneurs who recombine existing institutional materials to generate locally tailored growth strategies. This reconceptualization extends the literature on China’s united front system and contributes to broader debates on local state developmentalism and institutional entrepreneurship.

title: Governance causality theory: A causal architecture for regulatory and administrative systems
abstract: This theoretical manuscript develops a causal architecture for governance and administrative systems, explaining how authority, regulation, and institutional design interact to produce administrative outcomes. It forms part of a thesis‑by‑publication on governance architecture and delegated authority. The paper proposes a structural model linking causal mechanisms of rule formation, execution‑time authority, and institutional adaptation within public administration.

title: Disentangling Repetition and Imitation Components of Preferential Attachment in Social Action Networks
abstract: Models of social action networks frequently find that preferential attachment -- the recurring pattern in which individuals or entities that already have many social ties tend to attract even more over time -- is a powerful predictor of social interaction over time. The most common explanation of preferential attachment is social imitation, i.e., selecting interaction targets based on how others have selected targets in the past. However, the most commonly used measure of preferential attachment, target indegree, conflates two distinct mechanisms: social imitation, in which actors mimic behaviors they observe in others, and self-repetition, in which actors repeat their own past behaviors. Drawing from classical sociological theory, this paper proposes a conceptual and empirical distinction between these processes and demonstrates that they exhibit different sensitivities to features of the interaction environment. Using a hierarchical relational event model across a corpus of hundreds of social action networks, we show that models separating imitation and repetition provide improved fit and more interpretable results. In addition, while social imitation varies systematically with interaction visibility in the social ecology, repetition remains significant and positive when interaction visibility is low. The findings clarify the behavioral foundations of preferential attachment and provide conceptual precision in modeling sequential social dynamics across social ecologies.

title: Toward a Generative Theory of Social Structure: How Social Feedback and Reputation Produce Norms, Power, and Meaning
abstract: How do behavioral norms stabilize, shared interpretive frameworks consolidate, and power hierarchies form and reproduce — all without central coordination, prior agreement, or external enforcement, and all from the ordinary dynamics of everyday interaction? This paper presents a generative answer: a single recursive feedback mechanism, formalized as the socially responsive agent (SRA) model, from which all of these structures emerge endogenously, without external enforcement, pre-existing normative orders, or tradition-specific auxiliary assumptions. The mechanism is compact. Agents monitor symbolically mediated social feedback and adapt their behavior to increase their reputational standing within a reference group. The iterated operation of this loop, extended by gossip networks and imitation of opinion leaders, generates behavioral norms, shared schemas, and stable power hierarchies as natural consequences rather than as independent phenomena. These structures are unified within the concept of the social reward economy, which produces cohesion, polarization, resistance, and manipulation as structural outcomes of the same feedback dynamics. The theoretical stakes extend further. Mapped onto the SRA mechanism, the core contributions of Mead, Giddens, Bourdieu, Habermas, Luhmann, and Coleman appear not as incommensurable rivals but as accounts of different components of the same generative process. Most consequentially, the Habermas–Luhmann debate — long treated as an irresolvable confrontation between incompatible social ontologies — turns out to describe two empirically distinct regimes of the same underlying mechanism: one prevailing before opinion leadership consolidates, one after. What appeared philosophical becomes an empirical question. The paper develops this argument as a unified whole, drawing on work developed in its companion papers.

title: Healing Beyond the Table: The Role of Trauma and Gender in Mediation
abstract: Peace negotiations frequently collapse not because of failed diplomacy, but because of unaddressed trauma and the systematic exclusion of women. This policy analysis examines two critical dimensions of sustainable peacebuilding — trauma-informed practice and gender-inclusive participation — through comparative case studies of Rwanda and South Sudan. Rwanda's Gacaca courts illustrate both the healing potential and the re-traumatization risks of community-based truth-telling, while South Sudan's repeated peace failures expose the costs of excluding women and ignoring psychosocial wounds. Drawing on the Women, Peace and Security (WPS) framework anchored in UN Security Council Resolution 1325, this piece argues that durable peace requires mediation processes that are simultaneously trauma-conscious and gender-sensitive. Five concrete recommendations are offered for mediators, policymakers, and international organizations.

title: Hardwarism and biofoundry imaginaries in South Korea
abstract: International proponent literature legitimates biofoundries (the automated infrastructure of synthetic biology) through democratization rhetoric. That register has bifurcated since 2015 and faced a parallel strategic-asset register since 2022. How Korean stakeholders who shape biofoundry governance position themselves in this fragmenting register has not been measured. A fifteen-expert Korean panel evaluated four candidate framings against an anchoring interview with a senior researcher at a national biofoundry centre. A four-corpus parallel inspection (Korean policy texts, international policy and proponent-community documents, outward-facing Korean publications, civic-bioethics archive material across three sub-strata) and a Singapore illustrative vignette converge on the panel finding. Among the four offered framings, eight of fifteen mark bio-democratization most problematic while eleven endorse the instrumentalist bio-factory framing as most useful. The democratization lexicon appears in Korean civic-society discourse but disappears in statutory and biofoundry-policy registers, and every panelist-articulated alternative preserves the producer-consumer schema rather than the co-producer alternative. We read the pattern as consistent with Kim's (2011) hardwarism, the Korean discursive structure that treats technology as hardware produced by experts and adopted by users, a reading licensed for cross-national testing rather than within-panel proof. The Korean case sits in a developmental-state pathway, distinct from the UK pathway of attenuated democratization framings and from the post-2022 international pivot to strategic-asset framing. The paper contributes a within-group check protocol for stakeholder-consultation design and a policy diagnosis. Democratization vocabulary will not deliver the participatory legitimacy it promises, whereas an accountability-and-interoperability vocabulary aligned with the bio-factory framing does more analytical work.

title: Systematic Evaluation of Nation-State Propaganda in LLM Outputs
authors: Kevin T. Greene, Jacob N Shapiro
abstract: The rapid growth and usability of AI is creating new risks to information quality, including the possibility that chatbot responses spread propaganda from state-backed influence campaigns. There is little systematic evidence regarding how frequently LLMs spread such narratives when responding to user queries. Previous attempts to assess these risks rely on small numbers of evaluation questions, use opaque query construction procedures, and fail to account for key sources of uncertainty in model responses. We address these gaps with an automated, reproducible pipeline that samples documented state-backed propaganda and news items, programmatically generates evaluation questions, and audits LLM-generated responses. We evaluate whether five prominent models: (1) reproduce arguments aligned with documented Kremlin propaganda narratives; (2) present the Kremlin perspective on events without providing corrective context; and (3) cite known propaganda outlets. We find substantially lower rates of propaganda-aligned responses and propaganda-source citation than prior small-sample work would suggest. These risks are particularly rare for questions resembling ordinary information searches about Ukraine. Our approach enables systematic comparisons across models and issue areas, supports ongoing monitoring, and could be extended to many other hotly-debated topics.

title: Navigating the Fractured Frontier: Legal Risks in Vietnam’s International Financial Centers and Strategic Pathways for Global and Vietnam Investors
abstract: Vietnam’s International Financial Centers (IFCs) in Ho Chi Minh City and Danang represent a strategic pivot toward deep integration into the global financial architecture. Established under Resolution 222/2025/QH15, these centers promise flexible legal frameworks, preferential tax regimes, and specialized dispute resolution mechanisms. Yet, implementation reveals both visible and hidden legal risks that can undermine predictability and investment efficacy. Employing doctrinal legal analysis, comparative institutional benchmarking, and contrarian critique, this paper dissects two risk strata: visible risks (ambiguous legal status, fragmented governance, foreign exchange controls, and AML/CFT gaps) and hidden risks (enforcement discretion, judicial conservatism, cross-border data sovereignty conflicts, and geopolitical friction). Challenging the traditional “legal enclave” paradigm, the study argues that IFC success depends on systemic alignment with Vietnam’s domestic legal ecosystem rather than institutional isolation. The paper proposes a strategic architecture encompassing hybrid dispute resolution, dynamic contracting with stabilization clauses, co-regulatory dialogue, and climate-aligned ESG compliance. Its primary contribution lies in a multi-layered legal risk taxonomy and a paradigm shift from risk mitigation toward adaptive institutional co-creation, offering actionable frameworks for multinational enterprises, legal practitioners, and policymakers navigating emerging market financial hubs.

title: Reconsidering the Relationship Between Intelligence and Right-Wing Authoritarianism and Social Dominance Orientation: Evidence from a Discordant Twin Analysis
authors: Mirko Ruks, Christian Kandler, Jana Instinske, Edward Bell
abstract: Previous research has consistently shown that higher intelligence (IQ) is linked to lower levels of Right-Wing Authoritarianism (RWA) and Social Dominance Orientation (SDO). However, prior studies have been largely correlational, leaving it unclear to what degree these associations are driven by unobserved confounding. This study addresses this gap using longitudinal data from 6144 twins in the German TwinLife survey and employing a discordant-twin design. We estimated latent-variable within--between models, which allowed us to estimate the effect of IQ on RWA and SDO four years later while accounting for unobserved genetic and familial confounding. The pooled results replicate the negative associations reported in prior work, but the within-family estimates using identical twins show that these associations are substantially inflated by unobserved confounding. Specifically, the link between IQ and RWA appears to be partly driven by common genetic influences, whereas the association between IQ and SDO is primarily confounded by shared environmental factors. These findings suggest that the role of cognitive ability in fostering more liberal attitudes is often overstated and underscore the value of genetically informed designs in political psychology.

title: La paradoja del bienestar emprendedor: una revisión narrativa sobre autonomía, estrés y desempeño
abstract: El bienestar emprendedor se ha consolidado como un campo relevante para comprender el emprendimiento más allá de sus resultados económicos. Esta revisión narrativa sintetiza literatura científica publicada hasta el 31 de diciembre de 2023 sobre la relación entre emprendimiento, bienestar psicológico, estrés, desempeño, persistencia y sostenibilidad del negocio. El argumento central es que el emprendimiento produce una paradoja de bienestar: puede ofrecer autonomía, propósito, aprendizaje, identidad y satisfacción, pero también generar incertidumbre financiera, sobrecarga laboral, conflicto vida-trabajo, miedo al fracaso, agotamiento y deterioro de la salud mental. La revisión organiza la literatura en tres partes. Primero, se presentan los fundamentos conceptuales del bienestar emprendedor, integrando bienestar subjetivo, bienestar eudaimónico, salud mental, satisfacción laboral y equilibrio vida-trabajo. Segundo, se analizan factores que fortalecen o deterioran el bienestar, incluyendo autoeficacia, resiliencia, capital psicológico, pasión, apoyo familiar, redes, demandas laborales, género y contexto institucional. Tercero, se examinan las consecuencias del bienestar sobre desempeño, innovación, toma de decisiones, persistencia, salida del negocio, fracaso y recuperación. Se propone un modelo conceptual que entiende el bienestar emprendedor como resultado dinámico de la interacción entre demandas, recursos, mecanismos psicológicos, resultados empresariales y moderadores contextuales. El artículo concluye que el bienestar debe considerarse una dimensión central de la sostenibilidad emprendedora, especialmente en contextos latinoamericanos donde la informalidad, la desigualdad, el emprendimiento por necesidad y la limitada protección social pueden intensificar la vulnerabilidad de quienes emprenden. Palabras clave: bienestar emprendedor; emprendimiento; estrés; salud mental; desempeño; revisión narrativa.

title: Moral Regulation in Sequential Decisions
authors: Umberto Galmarini, Astrid Gamba, Ibai Martínez-Macías
abstract: Do individuals become more generous after harming others, or less generous after doing the right thing? We study whether moral behavior spills over across sequential decisions through moral accounting: individuals may offset prior moral debts through subsequent prosocial behavior (moral cleansing) or draw on prior moral credits to justify lower generosity (moral licensing). In an online experiment, participants first make a fair or unfair allocation in a Dictator Minigame. They then learn whether the Receiver’s payoff was determined by their own choice or by chance, and make an unanticipated decision about whether to donate part of their earnings to a charity. By varying responsibility for realized social outcomes, the design generates different moral states associated with the same first-stage choice, which can trigger compensatory behavior in the subsequent donation decision. We find a sharp asymmetry. After choosing the fair allocation, being responsible for the Receiver’s favorable outcome significantly reduces subsequent donations, consistent with moral licensing. After choosing the unfair allocation, responsibility has no average effect on giving, but this null effect conceals substantial heterogeneity in individual responses. Overall, the results show that responsibility for outcomes can shape later prosocial behavior, but does so asymmetrically across good and bad deeds.

title: Who’s afraid of relativism?
abstract: Speakers on both the political right and left often suggest that their opponents are engaged in, or given aid and comfort by, “relativism” or “postmodernism,” a rejection of universal standards of evidence, reason, or morality. Such rejection is taken to result in a collapse of norms into nihilism, reducing politics to brute propaganda and power play. Drawing on the philosophical tradition of American pragmatism, I argue that “relativism” is a boogeyman fear, and that rejection of the idea of universal standards does not make discourse, argument, or politics any less based in evidence, reason, or morality than they already were. I suggest that both accusations of relativism and their corollary claims of transcendent, universal justification are unproductive, overheated distractions from the difficult and pressing work of democratic deliberation, negotiation, and compromise.

title: 100 ANOS DA REVOLUÇÃO RUSSA: ENSINAMENTOS DA ATUAÇÃO DOS ANARQUISTAS
authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Fernando Antonio da Costa Vieira
abstract: O presente artigo procura apresentar, nos 100 anos de comemoração da Revolução Russa, uma análise histórica da participação anarquista nos processos pré-revolução e durante os anos de Comunismo de Guerra. Em um segundo momento, a pesquisa busca descrever o principal movimento anarquista durante a Revolução Russa, ou seja, a atuação de Nestor Ivánovitch Makhno e seu Exército Negro na Ucrânia. Por fim, o estudo almeja fazer uma correlação dos fatos históricos vividos naquele período, pelos grupos de esquerda, com a atual conjuntura brasileira.

title: “The seasons are the cornerstone of health”: The interconnection between local Aboriginal seasons and traditional foods on the wellbeing of Aboriginal peoples in the West Kimberley, Australia
authors: theoni whyman, John Jacky, Janine McNamara, Belinda Neo, Brad Farrant, Eleanor Dunlop, Carrington Shepherd, Lucinda J Black
abstract: Background: Traditional foods refer to native foods to Australia and have been consumed by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples over millennia. Traditional Aboriginal diets are high in protein, fibre and essential vitamins and mineral and are low in sugar, salt and saturated fat. The health benefits associated with traditional food consumption have been documented over several decades. However, access to and consumption of these traditional foods has declined for many Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples across Australia. Objective: This study explored Aboriginal people’s stories and perspectives relating to traditional foods, and examined factors influencing their access and consumption among communities in the West Kimberley region of Australia. Methods: The project used a Community Based Participatory Research methodology and was conducted in partnership with two Aboriginal communities. A key group from each community provided governance, cultural guidance and feedback throughout the project. Qualitative interviews were conducted using yarning, a culturally appropriate Aboriginal form of communication. A total of 22 Aboriginal community members aged 30 years and over participated in an individual or group yarn discussing their knowledge and experiences of local traditional foods and food practices. Results: Two main themes were identified: (1) traditional foods as central to Social and Emotional Wellbeing; and (2) factors influencing the hunting, gathering, and consumption of traditional foods. Conclusions: This project highlights the beneficial impact of traditional foods and the local Aboriginal seasons on the holistic health of Aboriginal peoples. Policies and programs aimed at improving nutrition, wellbeing, and food security should incorporate and support access to traditional foods and related practices.

title: Americans Favor Predistributive Over Redistributive Economic Policies
authors: David Broska, Jonne Kamphorst, Robb Willer
abstract: Redistributive policies designed to reduce economic inequality through taxes and transfers (e.g., safety-net benefits funded by progressive taxation) receive less support in the U.S. than in many Western democracies. Recent work suggests Americans may be more receptive to “predistributive” policies, which seek to reduce economic inequality before taxes and transfers (e.g., minimum-wage increases). Yet existing evidence leaves open whether this support gap characterizes contemporary Americans overall, and whether it persists when the same policy proposals are described in predistributive versus redistributive terms, with their core provisions held constant. Across 31 nationally representative U.S. survey waves fielded from 2015 to 2024 (total N=384,248), we find greater support for policies classified as predistributive than redistributive. We then fielded a preregistered experiment with a national U.S. sample (N=1,009), randomly varying whether otherwise identical policy proposals were described as increasing earnings for people lower in the income distribution, shifting resources from higher- to lower-income individuals, or presented only with the brief policy description given in all conditions. Support was higher under predistributive than redistributive descriptions. Comparison with the control condition clarifies this difference: predistributive descriptions received support comparable to control, whereas redistributive descriptions received lower support. Exploratory analyses suggest perceived respect for hard work and deservingness of beneficiaries were associated with the support gap. Together, these results suggest Americans prefer policies that reduce inequality by influencing earnings more than through taxes and transfers.

title: Domiciliary care for people living with dementia: a systematic review of stakeholder perspectives
authors: Ilianna Lourida, Elisabeth Grey, Katie Breheny, Rasa Mikelyte, Christopher Poyner
abstract: Background: Most people living with dementia prefer to remain at home and as care needs increase, they often require domiciliary support. Demand for homecare services is growing internationally but quality varies widely, and what is understood as good quality care for people living with dementia at home remains unclear. Understanding perspectives of key stakeholders in homecare is essential to inform policy and practice. We conducted a systematic review to synthesise the perspectives of people living with dementia, their informal carers, homecare workers and managers to understand what constitutes good domiciliary care and the factors that enable or constrain its delivery. Methods: We followed a systematic review approach guided by recommendations for rapid review methods. Systematic searches of MEDLINE, PsycINFO and CINAHL (September 2024) were supplemented by reference list checking of included studies. Screening, data extraction and quality appraisal using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool were performed by one reviewer with partial verification by a second reviewer. Studies were prioritised for conceptual contribution and text richness and analysed using thematic synthesis. Results: Ninety-seven studies met the inclusion criteria and 31 were prioritised for synthesis. Stakeholders consistently described good care as individualised and relationship-centred, promoting agency and participation of people with dementia and reducing informal carer burden. Homecare experiences highlighted interpersonal and organisational factors required to deliver such care including dementia knowledge and collaboration with families, continuity of care, service flexibility and organisational support for workers. However, systemic influences showed how rigid commissioning, task-focused care, fragmented communication and workforce casualisation constrained best practice, limiting opportunities for relationship-building and personalised care. Conclusions: Good domiciliary care includes individualised, relationship-centred support for people living with dementia and their carers, yet everyday practice is complex and often constrained by interpersonal, organisational and systemic factors. Without organisational conditions that sustain skilled and consistent care alongside broader systemic change, homecare workers cannot deliver the care they aspire to and families expect. Addressing these influences is important for workforce stability and retention and for delivering care that aligns with principles of good dementia care.

title: Weights for the German Longitudinal Environmental Study (GLEN)
authors: Lisa Lessner, Christiane Bozoyan, Claudia	Schmiedeberg
abstract: This report documents the construction and characteristics of the weights developed for the first five surveys of the German Longitudinal Environmental Study (GLEN). GLEN provides adjustment weights calibrated to 2024 data from official statistics (Microcensus and population update data). The report outlines the methodological decisions made in calculating the weights and describes their intended use for analyses based on the first GLEN Release.

title: Men Request More Funding than Women in Grant Applications
abstract: Science funding is the key to a sustainable innovation system. Yet, prior research documents systematic gender gap in funding amounts, which may emerge at the evaluation or application stage. Here, we study the latter mechanism by examining the funding amount requested by principal investigators (PI). Using a nationally representative sample from the Novo Nordisk Foundation covering 13,520 grant applications submitted between 2015 and 2022, we find that men PI request 25% more total budget than women PI. In addition, the gender gap increases over time and is greater among early-career PIs from the largest research area in medical sciences. This gender difference is robust to stringent controls for PI’s past experience, content novelty, linguistic features, and critical fixed-effect factors. Furthermore the gender gap persists when comparing applicants matched on observable confounding variables. Through mediation analysis, we identify three key mechanisms, including differences in presentation style, novelty preference, and research experience, which collectively explain 24% of the gender gap in funding requests. Importantly, we find that gender differences in requested amounts largely contribute to the gender gap in funded amounts. Our findings highlight the importance of understanding endogenous human behaviors in generating measurable gender inequality in funding outcomes, which provides a starting point for future work to dissect the origins of gender differences in formulating grant applications. We discuss practical interventions to mitigate the gender disparity in funding requests.

title: The Geopolitical Displacement of Global Capital and the New Zealand Safe Haven Logic: A Critique of the Active Investor Plus (AIP) Visa Ecosystem
authors: Yusuf Kamal, Taufeeq Rashid Pirzada, Amitabh Roy
abstract: This academic commentary investigates the Geopolitical Displacement (GD) of international wealth and the emergence of New Zealand (NZ) as a preeminent safe haven for global capital. Driven by a rigorous Safe Haven Logic (SHL), High-Net-Worth Individuals (HNWI) are increasingly redirecting funds from volatile regions, specifically citing the United Arab Emirates regional fluctuations and Political or Social Instability in the United States, toward the New Zealand luxury real estate sector? The study deconstructs the Structural Liberalisation of the Active Investor Plus Visa, which lowered investment thresholds and introduced a strategic NZD 5 million residential property exemption, effectively creating an isolated ultra-luxury tier. The paper also Analyses the Trans-Tasman Vacuum (TTV) created by the abolition of the Australian Significant Investor Visa (SIV), positioning New Zealand as a primary indirect substitute for mobile wealth. Despite these economic gains, the commentary identifies an Ethical Paradox regarding the Minimum Physical Presence requirement of only 21 days, questioning whether the Programme facilitates genuine social integration or merely provides Residency Insurance (RI) for the global elite. The analysis concludes that while the Active Investor Plus ecosystem has successfully captured nearly NZD 4billion in committed funds, its long-term durability depends on balancing global capital attraction with national social integrity.

title: Global fuel shocks as catalysts of sustainable travel behaviour change
authors: Lauren Pearson, Ana Luiza Santos de Sa, Robyn Gerhard, Jamie Abrahams, Hossein Nosratzadeh, Toby Cumming, Ben Beck
abstract: A sharp rise in fuel prices in March 2026 created a potential window for sustainable travel behaviour change in Australia. Yet who is positioned to change how they travel remains poorly understood. A population-representative online survey of 2,177 Australian adults, conducted in April 2026, captured pre-crisis travel behaviour, mode shift considerations, and conditions supporting sustained change. Explainable machine learning (XGBoost, SHAP, supervised k-means clustering) identified amenable subgroups for walking, cycling, e-bikes, and public transport. Three quarters of respondents had changed or considered changing their travel behaviour. Walking was the most commonly adopted new mode (20%), and travel avoidance the most common adaptation (41%). Financial hardship and younger age were dominant predictors of amenability. Supervised clustering revealed three recurrent archetypes - financially pressured young adults, employed urban professionals, and regional residents lacking service access. Realising equitable sustainable travel outcomes requires transport systems offering affordable, safe alternatives to all population groups.

title: The Paradox of Innocence: An Empirical Phenomenological Analysis on Juvenile Delinquency from Non-Criminogenic Families
abstract: This study examines pathways to juvenile delinquency among adolescents from non-criminogenic family backgrounds, addressing gaps in legal psychology that overemphasize familial criminality. Using a qualitative phenomenological design, semi-structured interviews were conducted with eight adolescents in a government rehabilitation facility. Data were analyzed using Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis, Colaizzi’s method, and systematic coding. Findings indicate that delinquency arises from interactions between developmental disruptions and socio-environmental influences, including familial disconnection, limited social integration, unstructured routines, and peer, media, and substance exposure. These factors intersect with emotional dysregulation, moral disengagement, and identity confusion. The study proposes an Exogenous-Endogenous Dynamic Model and an intervention framework.

title: Presidential Rhetoric and Approval: A Preregistered Test Using Embedding-Based Construct Measurement
abstract: Recent work has replicated Zullow and Seligman's (1990) finding that presidential-candidate optimism predicts election outcomes (Blyler et al., 2026). The present preregistered study examines a broader proposition: whether a nine-construct embedding-based framework of presidential rhetoric — spanning optimism, humility, affiliative humor, cooperation, pessimism, fear-mongering, defensiveness, aggression, and antagonism — predicts within-administration approval change. 470,995 speaker-turn chunks from U.S. presidential news conferences and press briefings, 1945–2025, were scored via cosine similarities between text-embedding-3-large vectors and per-construct centroids built from psychological-scale anchors; the anchor set was validated by two large language models (Cohen's κ .71–1.00). Preregistered multilevel models found no relationship between net rhetorical valence and subsequent approval at 7-, 14-, or 28- day lags; the null was robust to outlier trimming, leave-one-administration-out resampling, and a linearity check. The nine constructs collapse to one latent dimension at daily aggregation (r = −.98). Exploratory analyses surfaced a base-mobilization pattern: Antagonism positively and Optimism negatively predicted co-partisan approval. Within this multi-dimensional framework, presidential rhetoric does not appear to predict daily approval change.

title: CONSCIOUS COST_A Necessary Condition for Sustainable Social Relations
abstract: Abstract This paper presents findings from an autoethnographic investigation into the structural causes of relational breakdown, proposing "Conscious Cost" (Cs) — the degree to which an individual consciously recognizes the energy, time, and emotional burden invested in social relations — and advancing the following proposition: The recognition of Conscious Cost (Cs) is a necessary condition for sustainable social relations. Most relational breakdowns arise not from malice but from asymmetric cost recognition: one party invests significantly while failing to acknowledge that investment, producing a growing gap between actual cost (Cq) and recognized cost (Cs). This Gap = Cq − Cs — a structurally significant and under-recognized mechanism — generates resentment, burnout, and reciprocity collapse. The paper formalizes this mathematically; distinguishes Conscious Cost from Emotional Labor, the IKEA Effect, and the Just World Belief; and introduces the Reference Scale (R), the breadth of relational context against which individuals measure self-worth. Eight structural forces systematically shrink R, producing Cs distortion at scale. A four-quadrant framework (R × Cs) maps relational states and intervention pathways, grounded in an autoethnographic case study in which R expansion produced measurable shifts in Cs and reciprocity. Cs distortion is not individual failure but a structurally produced condition requiring structural design. Keywords: Conscious Cost; Qualitative Cost; Reference Scale; reciprocity; relational breakdown; emotional labor; autoethnography; self-worth independence; burnout; social relations AI Disclosure: The author used Claude (Anthropic) as a writing and research assistance tool in the preparation of this manuscript. All theoretical concepts, original insights, and intellectual contributions are the author's own.

title: Methodological Trend in Child Social Work Research in the Age of AI: a Five-Year Review
abstract: No study has systematically examined how data analysis methods have evolved in child and family social work research. This study analyzed 6,956 articles published between 2021 and 2025 in five SSCI-indexed journals (Child Abuse & Neglect, Child Maltreatment, Children and Youth Services Review, Family Relations, and Child and Adolescent Social Work Journal) to identify methodological trends coinciding with the emergence of generative artificial intelligence. Article abstracts were classified by study design and analytical approach using LLM-assisted content analysis, validated against human coding (Cohen's κ = .92 for study design, κ = .85 for analytical methods). Logistic regression models controlling for journal revealed significant increases in network analysis (OR = 1.851), machine learning (OR = 1.332), mediation analysis (OR = 1.075), and qualitative methods (OR = 1.078), alongside declines in bivariate analysis (OR = 0.954) and logistic regression (OR = 0.920). Pre- versus post-ChatGPT comparisons and sensitivity analyses confirmed these patterns. Findings document a shift toward more complex quantitative methods and growing methodological diversification, with implications for doctoral training, editorial standards, and the responsible use of AI in research.

title: Coming into Being with Place
abstract: Driven by the question, “what is the nature of being in place?”, the authors explore their personal experiences and how they came to understand place as more than an object or setting, but essential relations that shape the very rhythms of life, their personal identities, social rituals, and physical bodies. Using a narrative approach, the authors explore what is revealed about relationship between people and places when viewed through an Indigenous-inspired relational worldview rather than a Western materialist worldview. The authors introduce the Kithship Wheel, a visual lens that depicts three interlocking elements (person, people, and place) connected by four relational processes: valuing, being, knowing, and doing. The wheel is meant to help readers see how emotions, cultural narratives, and everyday practices co-constitute identity and belonging, offering a way to understand and deepen one’s sense of place beyond mainstream concepts in conventional sense of place research.

title: A Historical Analysis of the Squatter Settlements (Sukumbasi) in Nepal and the Role of Smart Settlements
abstract: The Sukumbasi question in Nepal must be understood as a historically layered problem of inequality, social displacement, legal ambiguity, and unstable state intervention, rather than a narrow or recent problem of informal settlement. Much of the policy discourse has treated Sukumbasi as an issue related to encroachment, unauthorized occupation, or land administration problem. Such a framing is analytically insufficient. The historical record suggests that the problem emerged through a lengthy process in which landlessness was first embedded within older systems of dependency, then made visible through the abolition of slavery, later managed through settlement and resettlement schemes, and eventually formalized through commissions, legal definitions, and statutory categories. Across successive political regimes, the Nepali state repeatedly acknowledged the problems of landlessness and created mechanisms to address it. Yet these interventions rarely matured into durable solutions. (De Schutter, 2021). The central argument of this historical review is that the Sukumbasi question in Nepal is not best explained by policy absence, but by the proliferation of public agencies without institutional continuity. The state has repeatedly identified the landless, classified them, collected applications for land allocation, and announced redistributive or regularizing measures. However, commissions have been short-lived, legal categories of Sukumbasi have shifted in scope, implementation has been interrupted, and institutional memory has repeatedly been fractured. Consequently, the history of the Sukumbasi issue is less a story of the invisibility of the settlers than one of repeated recognition followed by recurring suspension (De Schutter, 2021).

title: Exploring the determinants of men’s involvement in group walking: A demonstrative network modelling study.
authors: Brendan Smith, Eleanor Quested, Joanne A McVeigh, Matthew McDonald, Dominika Kwasnicka, Janelle Woods, Elizabeth Calleja, Hugh Riddell
abstract: Background: Men in Australia are more likely than women to present with risk factors for coronary heart disease and are underrepresented in community-based physical activity (PA) initiatives. Understanding the characteristics of those who do participate can help identify groups not being reached. Traditional statistical approaches may not capture the complex interrelationships between demographic and behavioural factors that shape participation. This exploratory study demonstrates the utility of network modelling for public health research by identifying characteristics associated with men’s involvement in The National Heart Foundation Australia’s Heart Foundation Walking (HFW) groups program and examining how these characteristics interrelate. Methods: Exploratory network modelling was conducted using complete data from 30,909 participants (19.3% male; mean age=63.4, SD=14.38, range=18-96years). Variables included demographics (age, gender, socioeconomic status), location, enrolment year, role (organiser vs member) and self-reported PA at enrolment. Conditional dependencies among these variables were modelled to identify patterns associated with male participation. Results: Compared to women, men at enrolment were older (mean age=65 vs 63), self-reported meeting PA guidelines (44% vs 37%), and were more often organisers (2.3% vs 1.7%). Role was the most central variable, showing the strongest associations with others, while gender had lower centrality and was positioned peripherally. Self-report PA acted as a bridge node connecting characteristics. Conclusions: Network analysis visualised complex relationships among participant characteristics, revealing patterns not captured by traditional approaches. This method provides a tool for generating hypotheses about participation in community-based health initiatives. Further research should explore men’s experiences in HFW-groups to guide practical strategies for engagement.
